question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) any steps her Department has taken to prepare for the implementation of the fundamental review of criminal legal aid framework; (ii) whether the recommendations will be implemented in full; and (iii) a timeframe for implementation.

To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 40257/22-27, to detail (i) any assessment that has been made on whether existing manslaughter offences can be difficult to prove; (ii) any assessment that has been made on whether a specific offence of domestic abuse-induced suicide would provide greater clarity to police, prosecutors, judges and juries; and (iii) the number of recorded manslaughter convictions that have occurred in cases involving suicide, over the last 15 years.
To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the multi-year budget process.

To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will introduce legislation, similar to Jade's Law, which would automatically suspend parental responsibility for parents who murder their partner or ex-partner.
answer_text: Whilst I have huge sympathy for the policy intent of Jade’s Law, the policy surrounding parental rights and family law is the responsibility of the Department of Finance. I therefore do not have plans to introduce legislation similar to Jade’s Law.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the circumstances required for her Department to expand the Domestic Homicide Review scheme, to include death by suicide.
To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any work being undertaken by her Department to implement an enhanced needs assessment service, within the Victim and Witness Care Unit.
answer_text: The new Victims and Witnesses of Crime Strategy which I launched on 11 March reflects my Department’s commitment to working towards the establishment of a Victim Needs Assessment Service (VNAS) and some preparatory work has already commenced to this effect.   In January 2026, multiagency scoping work was commenced and further development work in conjunction with key stakeholders including PSNI, PPS, and Victim Support NI, etc will continue throughout 2026 and 2027.  However, the operational establishment of a VNAS will be dependent on additional funding being made available. My Department has bid for funding under the Transformation Fund which, if met, would cover the cost of establishing a VNAS, with the intention of the service being operational from the second half of financial year 2027/28.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any jurisdictions that were considered by her officials during the drafting of the Criminal Justice (Sentencing etc.) Bill (26/22-27) provisions, for assaults on public workers.
answer_text: As part of the policy development process, the Department researched the approaches taken to this issue in England & Wales, Scotland and the Republic of Ireland.   A summary of the Department's findings was included at Chapter 8 of the Sentencing Review consultation document published on 28 October 2019, which is available at the following link - Sentencing Review  Northern Ireland - A Public Consultation.   The Department has continued to follow developments in these jurisdictions closely throughout the policy development process and the drafting of the Bill.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) a timeframe for a consultation on hate crime legislation; and (ii) whether the consultation will include proposals to tackle misogyny and sectarianism.
answer_text: The Phase 2 Consultation to include outstanding recommendations from Judge Marinnan’s review is currently paused until after the Criminal Justice (Sentencing etc.) Bill has passed through the Assembly. The Consultation will be progressed in the next mandate and will invite views on the addition of new protected characteristics of age and sex/gender. The Consultation may also seek the views of the public on provisions for a definition of sectarianism to accompany the introduction of a statutory sectarian aggravator.Policy work is continuing on assessing the suitability of the inclusion of sex/gender as a protected characteristic in hate crime legislation, including the consideration of misogyny offences within the hate crime framework, in preparation for the Phase 2 Consultation.The Department is also continuing to progress policy development on provisions for a sectarian aggravator in advance of the consultation. The Department’s priority is to ensure that the most impactful provisions to strengthen hate crime legislation will be delivered in this mandate and I remain committed to delivering the best outcomes for all victims of hate crime

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether any assessment has been made of the Criminal Justice Inspection NI Report of an Unannounced Inspection of Magilligan Prison 9-26 September 2025; and, (ii) to detail the rationale as to why the 2021 recommendations relating to access to GP appointments, psychological services, and addiction services remain unachieved.
answer_text: My Department welcomes publication of the very positive inspection report of Magilligan Prison. The report highlights good practice and areas of significant improvement since the last inspection.  However, I recognise that some areas still require further attention.  During 2023/24 my Department has committed an additional recurrent investment of almost £800k for healthcare in prison, with a particular focus on addressing mental health and substance use needs.  This has reduced the number of individuals waiting for Opiate Substitution Treatment in prison, although waiting times in prison remain longer than in the community. Given the rise in the prison population, which places an additional pressure on the healthcare services in prison, and the very challenging financial position the Department is experiencing it has not been possible to fully implement all recommendations.  My Department, along with the Public Health Agency, will continue to work with the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust to ensure healthcare services in prison are equivalent to those in the community setting.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on her Department's work to develop data metrics to monitor and evaluate the effectiveness of special measures for victims and witnesses of crime.
answer_text: I recognise the provision of special measures is key to supporting those most vulnerable through their journey in the criminal justice system.  Currently my department collates data on the use of Remote Evidence Centres and Registered Intermediaries, two important special measures, and some data is also collected by operational partners regarding the use of special measures.  In seeking to make improvements in relation to data collection, my department currently leads on a data monitoring working group comprising of various relevant stakeholders such as the PPS, PSNI, NICTS and voluntary and community sector partners etc. The purpose of this group is to develop and refine data metrics across the criminal justice system relating to victims and witnesses. This will enable effective monitoring and evaluation of compliance with Victim and Witness Charter Rights, including the provision of special measures.  Phase 1 of the Group’s work—an initial scoping exercise to identify gaps in existing data metrics—is now complete. Phase 2 is underway and involves collating the available data, consolidating it, and identifying a suitable dashboard to ensure information is presented clearly and accessibly for publication.  My current assessment is that we will also need to progress a further phase, both to identify actions needed to address any gaps in data collection and monitoring and to implement any necessary changes to IT systems to allow this data to be captured. Such changes, once identified, will need to be coordinated and progressed in the context of wider digital change priorities across the criminal justice system.In addition, I intend to introduce a Victims and Witnesses of Crime Bill to the Northern Ireland Assembly before summer recess. This legislation will include provisions that will require criminal justice agencies and service providers to report on their compliance with Victim and Witness Charter Rights, which includes those related to special measures.  My Department is currently working with operational partners to take forward preparatory work to facilitate this requirement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will undertake a review to consider the creation of a new criminal offence of femicide, in order to to reflect the prevalence of misogyny in homicides.
answer_text: The Department of Justice is firmly committed to tackling abuse and violence in all its forms.  The launch of the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy, together with the Executive’s Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls (EVAWG), reflects a comprehensive and coordinated approach to addressing these harms. While femicide is recognised as the most extreme manifestation of violence against women, the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy emphasises the need for a wider, inclusive framework that captures the full context in which domestic and sexual abuse occurs.At present, existing criminal law provides for all forms of homicide, including cases where misogyny or genderbased violence may be a factor. The Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act has further strengthened the justice response by introducing a statutory domestic abuse aggravator, ensuring that where an offence, including a femicide, is committed in a domestic context, the courts can impose a higher sentence to reflect the seriousness and underlying patterns of abusive, controlling, or coercive behaviour.Given this existing legislative framework, my priority is to ensure that femicide, and all forms of genderbased violence, are addressed robustly through strengthened prevention, protection, and support measures rather than through the creation of a new standalone offence. Embedding the issue within this wider strategic and legislative response enables government and partner agencies to capture the full spectrum of abuse, ensure consistent protection for all victims, and drive the systemic change needed to eradicate violence across society. However, I am also cognisant of the growing body of evidence which is available from jurisdictions which have specifically criminalised femicide and misogyny.  The Department will, therefore, continue to keep the effectiveness of current legislation under review but, at this time, I do not consider that the creation of a separate offence of femicide would provide additional protection beyond what the law already affords.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any assessment that has been made of the impact of the Strategic Framework to End Violence against Women and Girls.
answer_text: Ending violence against women and girls is one of the most pressing challenges facing our society today. That is why it is an Executive priority.The recent tragic murders of Ellie Flanagan and Amy Doherty remind us of the devastating consequences of violence against women and girls. It is unacceptable, and we will continue to do everything we can to stop it. We have invested over £5 million on the ground through the Local and Regional Change Funds, to raise awareness on violence against women and girls and equip everyone to take action to prevent it.  Early feedback shows strong community demand, high engagement and encouraging shifts in awareness which lay a solid foundation for scaling activity in the next delivery plan which we hope to issue shortly. We will continue to drive further actions by bringing together partners from key sectors including workplaces, safer socialising, and tertiary education to work collaboratively delivering new campaigns to raise awareness and help shift attitudes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in relation to the Education Authority review of its transport services, to outline how ISO 7176-19 is being implemented in terms of home to school transport for children with disabilities.
answer_text: The Education Authority is responsible of the operation and delivery of home to school transport, including making the necessary arrangements to support safe transport of pupils with disabilities.  The EA has advised me that ISO 717619 is an international safety standard applied by manufacturers to wheelchairs intended to be used as seats in motor vehicles.  This is not a new standard; however, the EA has encountered challenges where some manufacturers state that certain wheelchairs and restraint systems are not guaranteed for users with a very low body weight. Where a pupil’s weight falls below the limits specified by the manufacturer, the EA is unable to offer transport for the pupil seated in their wheelchair. In such circumstances, transport assistance may be provided by means of a parental allowance. The EA has advised that it is currently liaising with occupational therapists and the Public Health Agency to consider this issue and determine an appropriate way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update into service provision for post-19 adults with Learning Difficulties, Challenging Behaviour and Complex Needs within the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Service provision for adults aged 19 and over with Learning Difficulties, behaviours that may present as challenging and complex needs within Northern Health and Social Care Trust, is determined by an individual’s presenting need. There is no dedicated programme of care specifically for people with learning difficulties, as distinct from people with a diagnosed learning disability.Where complex needs relate to physical disability, sensory impairment or healthcare issues, the Northern Trust’s Physical Disability services can assess and provide appropriate interventions, including equipment, social care supports or onward referral. For individuals whose needs relate primarily to mental health, support is available through Northern Trust Community Mental Health teams.However, where people are assessed as not meeting the criteria for Learning Disability services, it can be the case that the pathways to alternative forms of support can be more unclear. For these individuals all Health Trusts, including Northern Health and Social Care Trust, apply professional discretion and ‘Best Fit’ principles to determine how a person’s needs can be met through locally available services.At a departmental level, work is underway to improve clarity and consistency and strengthen post-19 support. This includes work to review and update the Adult Autism Care Pathway, as well as ongoing analysis of responses to the recent public consultation on the draft Learning Disability Service Model. The Learning Disability Service Model promotes a personcentred, needsled approach and aims to ensure that adults with a diagnosed learning disability receive lifelong, coordinated support that is tailored to their individual circumstances and enables choice and control.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any humanitarian representations they have made to the Irish Government and British Government on behalf of local people who are in danger following the US Israeli-led military attacks in the Middle East.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the legislative timeline to progress the Inquiry (Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses) and Redress Scheme Bill (NIA Bill 15/22-27).

To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) whether the terms of reference for the accelerated review, regarding the Criminal Bar Association's withdrawal of services, have been agreed; (ii) whether co-leads have been appointed; and (iii) the timeline for the completion of the review.
answer_text: The remit of the joint leads and therefore their appointment must necessarily follow agreement on the scope of the accelerated review which is a matter of ongoing discussion with the Criminal Bar Association and Solicitors Criminal Bar Association, alongside draft terms of reference and overarching conditions of the review. Scope will also necessarily determine timescales for completion, but I expect work to proceed and conclude at pace and am deploying Departmental resources to that end. It is critical that the Criminal Bar Association quickly return to full service in the interests of the victims and witnesses who suffering additional stress and uncertainty as the result of the service withdrawal. They remain my focus and priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he has raised the issue of the 10 per cent wear and tear tax allowance with HMRC.
answer_text: The 10 per cent ‘wear and tear’ allowance relates to HMRC’s Making Tax Digital for Income Tax programme. This issue was first raised with me in correspondence last month, specifically in relation to the removal of the allowance for childminders with income of over £50,000, from April of this year. While affected childminders will still be able to claim tax relief on business expenses, there is concern from the sector on how the removal of this allowance might impact on some childminders, and the practicalities of how the new approach will be implemented. My officials have raised these issues directly with HMRC.I also understand that there had been no engagement with local sector representatives by HMRC until recently, and only following my department’s intervention. HMRC is now actively engaging with the sector locally, to discuss their concerns. My department has pressed HMRC on the importance of recognising and acting on any concerns raised by the sector, ahead of the changes coming into effect.My department has also been liaising with the Department of Education on this matter, given its responsibilities for childcare provision. I understand that Education officials are monitoring any impact the changes may have on childminder provision at a strategic level.  My officials will continue to engage with the Department of Education and HMRC to understand how the sector’s concerns are being responded to.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the rationale as to why deaths by suicide were not included in the scope of Domestic Homicide Reviews, when first introduced in 2021; and (ii) whether she will expand the scope of Domestic Homicide Reviews to include suicide deaths, where there is evidence of domestic abuse.
answer_text: Domestic Homicide Reviews (DHRs) were introduced in Northern Ireland in 2020. At that stage, the intention was to allow the system time to embed, generate learning, and understand the resource demands before considering an extension to include suiciderelated deaths. England and Wales took a similar approach: although DHRs became operational in that jurisdiction in 2011, suicides were not formally brought within scope until 2016. I fully recognise the strong link between domestic abuse and related suicides and want to acknowledge the devastation felt by all families who have lost loved ones in these circumstances. I remain committed to widening the scope of DHRs to include deaths by suicide. Suicide cases are, by their nature, highly complex.  Bringing them formally within the scope of DHRs raises several challenges. Establishing a clear and demonstrable link between domestic abuse and a suicide can be extremely difficult. Unlike domestic homicides, where the cause of death is directly attributable to another individual, domestic abuse related suicides often, but not always, involve multiple intersecting factors such as mental illhealth, trauma, substance misuse, and broader personal or social pressures. Determining whether domestic abuse was the primary contributing factor may, therefore, be subjective and challenging to evidence. Significant policy consideration will need to be given to how this can be achieved.I also have some concerns that expanding DHRs to include suicide cases would increase the number of reviews required to the point where the system could not cope without considerable additional investment by the Executive. Without additional statutory powers, resources, and specialist expertise, this would place further and significant pressure on services and organisations which are already stretched and could potentially dilute the focus on learning and affect both the timeliness and quality of reviews. The intent is not to place the importance or value of domestic abuse related suicides below that of domestic homicides, it is rather to explain the additional considerations involved.Despite these challenges, my Department remains committed to reviewing how deaths by suicide can be incorporated into the DHR process. Our aim is always to learn, to improve, and ultimately to better protect those at risk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of developments in England and Wales, where Domestic Homicide Reviews have been strengthened and renamed as Domestic Abuse Related Death Review.
answer_text: I understand that the Home Office is updating the statutory guidance for conducting domestic homicide reviews in England and Wales, including renaming these reviews as Domestic Abuse Related Death Reviews to reflect a broader understanding of the circumstances in which domestic abuse may contribute to a death. The updated guidance is expected to be published early this year alongside the related legislative changes.My Department’s officials remain in regular contact with colleagues across all UK jurisdictions and will continue to monitor developments closely as the updated guidance and accompanying legislative changes come into operation in England and Wales. The learning emerging from this work will help inform and strengthen future practice in Northern Ireland.I am mindful of the many grieving families and communities who have been left devastated by harms and deaths caused by domestic abuse, and my Department remains committed to ensuring we learn as much as possible from Domestic Homicide Reviews to help prevent future tragedies. I acknowledge that there are concerns that suicides do not currently fall within the scope of Domestic Homicide Reviews (DHRs) in Northern Ireland, and that this can limit the extent to which wider systemic learning is captured. I have previously indicated my intention to extend the existing DHR model to include domestic abuse-related death by suicide in due course. That remains my intention. However, this will require careful policy development and it could not be taken forward without significant additional resources be allocated by the Executive across the wider system and all relevant organisations. Without additional statutory powers, resources, and specialist expertise, expanding DHRs to include suicide cases would place further considerable pressure on services and organisations which are already stretched and could potentially dilute the focus on learning and affect both the timeliness and quality of reviews.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of existing legislation governing death by suicides, where domestic abuse has been found to be a contributing factor; and (ii) whether she will carry out a review of whether a new criminal offence of domestic abuse-induced suicide should be created.
answer_text: This is an extremely serious and sensitive matter. The devastating effects of domestic abuse on victims, including its potential role in contributing to suicide, must never be underestimated.While there is currently no specific offence in any part of the UK for causing or encouraging suicide through domestic abuse or coercive control, sections 13 and 13A of the Criminal Justice Act (Northern Ireland) 1966 make it an offence to encourage or assist the suicide or attempted suicide of another person, or to intend to do so. Section 13A also makes clear that this includes situations where a person has encouraged or assisted another by threatening them or otherwise pressuring them to commit or attempt suicide.The introduction of the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021 marked a significant milestone in strengthening the courts’ ability to protect individuals from violent and abusive behaviour. The Act has transformed how the criminal justice system understands and responds to domestic abuse. Protection is no longer limited to physical violence; the legislation also criminalises a course of abusive behaviour, including controlling or coercive conduct, that may be psychological, emotional, or financial.Under section 15 of the 2021 Act, any offence (other than the domestic abuse offence itself) may be aggravated if it involves domestic abuse. Where both the offence and the aggravation are proven, the court must consider the aggravation when determining the sentence. In principle, this could apply to manslaughter if it were established that a person died by suicide as a result of domestic abuse.I am satisfied that the current legislation gives the courts sufficient powers to hold perpetrators of domestic abuse to account. However, tackling domestic abuse in all its forms remains a key priority for my Department. My Department continues to engage with other departments and stakeholders, on how the justice system can better respond to domestic abuse and its consequences and I will continue to keep the law under review, drawing on emerging evidence and developments in neighbouring jurisdictions.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, following the establishment of the Office of AI and Digital, for their assessment of its impact on public sector transformation in terms of improving service delivery.
To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the criteria used to select projects in the most recent Your School, Your Club programme.
To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the benefits of a new statutory register of domestic abuse offenders, similar to the existing sex offenders register.
answer_text: I understand a register of domestic abuse offenders is being progressed in the Republic of Ireland but as I have stated before, my Department has no current plans to create a register of individuals convicted of domestic abuse.  Here in Northern Ireland, the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) has access to several national databases containing information on individuals with a history of offending and/or abusive behaviour. These include the Police National Computer (PNC), the Police National Database (PND), and the ViSOR, which records details of violent and sexual offenders. Collectively, these databases assist the police in identifying repeat or high-risk offenders and inform safeguarding decisions, including those made under the well-established Domestic Violence and Abuse Disclosure Scheme (DVADS) we have in Northern Ireland. The DVAD Scheme is intended to empower individuals who are at risk of domestic abuse to make informed decisions about their relationship and their safety.  The scheme provides a targeted, risk-based mechanism that allows the police to disclose relevant information about an individual’s history of domestic abuse where this is necessary to protect a potential victim. The scheme operates through both the “right to ask” by individuals and the police “power to tell” pathway, ensuring that disclosures are made only following a robust assessment of risk, proportionality, and necessity with the aim of keeping people safe.  It was developed by my Department and is operated by PSNI in conjunction with its statutory and voluntary partners.  That said, I remain committed to ensuring that the justice system offers robust protection and reassurance to those affected by domestic abuse.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to urgently address the lack of bus service for those who attend Magherafelt and Maghera day centres.
answer_text: The Northern Health and Social Care Trust (NHSCT) have provided the following response. The NHSCT continues to advertise for Driving Staff in the Magherafelt area.  To date, the Driving Posts have been advertised on 6 occasions over the last 6 months, without success.  Due to the lack of Bus Drivers in the Transport Industry at present, it is proving extremely difficult to recruit into a Band 3 Post.  The Health Service is in competition for Drivers within other sectors, such as the Education Board and private companies, offering higher rates of pay.The NHSCT continue to advertise and have purchased additional 9 seat minibuses, which will allow Care Staff, based at Adult/Day Centres to assist in bringing Service Users to the Centres.  This will supplement the existing transport.  These buses are due to be delivered end of February/beginning of March 2026.In addition to the above, Local Councils have been contacted previously to discuss the possibility of initiatives being offered to the unemployed to obtain their bus licence.  Unfortunately, this was unsuccessful.The NHSCT remains committed to the recruitment and retention of drivers to enable individuals to access Adult Day Centres.  Enabling individuals with learning disabilities to access more diverse and meaningful activity and transport, is an area of focus within the Learning Disability Service Model, currently being finalised by Department Officials, post public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of how the brokerage system in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust is working to secure packages of care for vulnerable people within their own homes.
answer_text: The NHSCT Brokerage system communicates required packages of care to providers up to three times per day. Providers respond via an online portal, resulting in timely allocation of provider to service user and minimising waits. During the month of January 2026, 565 packages of care were sourced in this way.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any service currently available for those with a diagnosis of Tourette syndrome, in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: In the Northern Health and Social Care Trust patients referred with Tourette Syndrome are seen within the Neurology Department. Treatments may involve referral for Cognitive Behavioural Treatment in the first instance as psychological treatment. In some cases, if required, a referral may be made to mental health services. Pharmacological management may also be considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any action his Department has taken to implement training on Tourette’s Syndrome, including any use of the video produced through the Education Authority with the support of Tourette's Support NI.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) Educational Psychology Service (EPS) provide advice to schools as part of their casework, based on evidence informed practice and their background training in neurodevelopmental conditions.At present, the EPS does not deliver any service wide, standalone training specifically on Tourette’s Syndrome, however, where Tourette’s is a feature of a child or young person’s profile, Educational Psychologists offer tailored guidance to schools on how best to support the pupil’s learning, wellbeing, and participation.Schools are directed to the video resource produced by Tourette’s Support Northern Ireland with the support of EA resources. This resource provides accessible information for staff and is used to help raise awareness and understanding within educational settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of journeys, in the last 12 months, which could not be delivered for users of the Magherafelt Adult Centre because of driver unavailability.
answer_text: The Northern Health and Social Care Trust (NHSCT) advise the Trust is unable to detail the number of journeys, in the last 12 months, which could not be delivered for service users of the Magherafelt Adult Centre because of driver unavailability; citing the Transport Department is unable to provide verified data relating to this request. The NHSCT remains committed to the recruitment and retention of drivers to enable individuals to access Adult Day Centres.  Enabling individuals with learning disabilities to access more diverse and meaningful activity and transport, is an area of focus within the Learning Disability Service Model, currently being finalised by Department Officials, post public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the introduction of Domestic Abuse Protection Orders and Notices.
answer_text: The introduction of Domestic Abuse Protection Notices (DAPN) and Domestic Abuse Protection Orders (DAPO) remains a priority for my Department in strengthening protections for victims of domestic abuse.Officials, in conjunction with PSNI colleagues, continue to progress policy and implementation planning, informed by learning from pilots in England and Wales and how this can most effectively be adapted to the Northern Ireland context. This work is being taken forward in close collaboration with key justice partners including support organisations to ensure that any future model is operationally robust and deliverable.As part of this engagement, discussions have commenced with the Lady Chief Justice’s Office, and engagement will shortly begin with colleagues in the Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service to consider operational and procedural implications for the judiciary and courts.Subject to the completion of this engagement, the finalisation of the preferred delivery model, and the availability of funding, my Department is working towards implementation of a pilot during the 2026–27 period. Work is ongoing to confirm the detailed timelines, cost implications, and any legislative or regulatory requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any criteria used by the Northern Health and Social Care Trust to diagnose a (i) child; or (ii) adult, with a personality disorder.
answer_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any criteria used by the Northern Health and Social Care Trust to diagnose a (i) child; or (ii) adult, with a personality disorder,Child HSC Trust CAMHS services apply both diagnostic frameworks and a biopsychosocial formulation approach when assessing young people, recognising that personality development is ongoing throughout adolescence, and that symptom overlap and developmental fluidity can make early diagnostic labelling problematic. In this context, a formulation-based approach is often more clinically meaningful, particularly for young people who have experienced trauma, instability or significant emotional turbulence, as it enables a more accurate understanding of need and supports the development of an appropriate care plan.While current clinical guidelines now permit the diagnosis of Borderline Personality Disorder (BPD) in individuals under 18, such consideration is reserved for cases where a diagnosis would clearly support access to specialised services or evidencebased interventions. AdultThe International Classification of Disease 10 (ICD 10) criteria is used as a framework to guide diagnosis.To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department has taken to support those with a family member with suspected personality disorder, who will not engage in the process for diagnosisWhile a diagnosis and treatment cannot be provided without the individual’s consent, HSC Trusts support carers in their own right through carer assessments, access to carers’ support services, and the provision of advice and information to help families understand complex emotional and behavioural difficulties and to manage risk and maintain wellbeing.  Where appropriate, safeguarding pathways and crisis planning advice are also discussed.Families are signposted to relevant voluntary and community sector organisations with expertise in supporting carers of people with complex mental health needs. Additionally, Recovery Colleges deliver training courses on both Personality Disorder and self-harm that are often attended by carers looking for some information on the diagnosis and how to support their loved one.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) his assessment of the existing accommodation options available for children who require a bail address; and (ii) any steps his Department is taking to increase accommodation capacity.
answer_text: The Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 (‘the Children Order’) places a statutory duty on Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts to safeguard and promote the welfare of children in need, including those in care.  As a Corporate Parent, the HSC Trust assumes the moral and legal responsibility for the wellbeing of a child in its care, including when a child becomes involved in the juvenile justice system.The Children Order also places specific duties on HSC Trusts to provide accommodation to children in care, eligible individuals who have left care and children for whom a responsible adult cannot provide accommodation.  In fulfilling these duties, they must ensure that, when required, a child has somewhere safe to live while on bail, that meets the bail conditions.  For those children who are in care, this is often the child’s current placement e.g. foster care or residential home.  However, as this cohort of children and young people often have highly complex needs / situations this is not always possible, and the Trust will need to identify an alternative placement which meets the needs of the child.  For those children and young people not already in care, they may become looked after so that the relevant HSC Trust can provide them with suitable accommodation while on bail.   My Department, along with the Youth Justice Agency (YJA), is involved in several forums to discuss and highlight various issues, individual cases, tends and data analysis between children and young people and interaction with the juvenile justice system.  These include, for example;Children and Young People’s Strategic Partnership (CYPSP) Regional Offending Sub-Group, The Partnership Board for Woodlands Juvenile Justice Centre and Lakewood Regional Secure Care Centre,The Regional Practice Network for Unaccompanied Asylum-Seeking Children and,The Strategic Framework for Youth Justice Implementation Group In addition, as part of the Children’s Social Care Services Strategic Reform Programme established to address the challenges which led to the Independent Review of Children’s Social Care, there is a specific focus on enhancing capacity and capability of our residential care provision, in particular through ongoing work to develop a regional strategic plan for children’s residential care.  The Youth Justice Agency is a member of the overall Reform Programme Board and is also represented in the Residential Capacity Workstream which is taking forward this work.  Discussions regarding bail addresses have also taken place in the Fostering Workstream of the Reform Programme, in particular in relation to bail accommodation in a specialist foster placement. The existing bail fostering initiative which has been piloted in the Southern Trust area aims to provide short-term therapeutic foster placements for children remanded to Woodlands Juvenile Justice Centre who could not perfect bail due to lack of suitable accommodation being available. question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the discussions to end the Criminal Bar Association's withdrawal of services.
answer_text: Engagement with the Bar Council and Criminal Bar Association has been ongoing since before the service withdrawal began and discussions continue. I met with representatives of the CBA again on 15 January 2026 when I proposed a means of navigating the current impasse which builds on work done to date. My proposed approach would both allow the concerns of the CBA to be addressed at pace and fulfil our requirements in terms of appropriately managing public money. The proposed approach will also involve the Solicitors Criminal Bar Association who have remained engaged and committed to the agreed process throughout the last year and who have ensured no impact on access to justice by also continuing to deliver services to their clients. I hope my proposed accelerated approach will result in a full return to service in the near future but it is a matter of intensive ongoing engagement and so it would not be appropriate to comment further at this stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the current waiting time for plastic surgery within the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Accredited Official Statistics for waiting times are published on a quarterly basis in the ‘Northern Ireland Outpatient Waiting Time Statistics’ and the ‘Northern Ireland Inpatient and Day Case Waiting Time Statistics’ bulletins, respectively.  The number of patients waiting for a first consultant-led outpatient appointment and the number of patients waiting for treatment in Adult and Paediatric Plastic Surgery are shown by HSC Trust, specialty, and time band in the accompanying data files. The most recent issues of these bulletins have a reporting date at 30 September 2025 and are available to view at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/outpatient-waiting-timeshttps://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/inpatient-waiting-timesThe next issues of the Outpatient and Inpatient and Day Case Waiting Time Statistics bulletins will have a reporting date of 31 December 2025 and will be published on 4 March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any discussions his Department has had with the Department of Justice in relation to the provision of appropriate accommodation for children who require a bail address.
answer_text: The Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 (‘the Children Order’) places a statutory duty on Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts to safeguard and promote the welfare of children in need, including those in care.  As a Corporate Parent, the HSC Trust assumes the moral and legal responsibility for the wellbeing of a child in its care, including when a child becomes involved in the juvenile justice system.The Children Order also places specific duties on HSC Trusts to provide accommodation to children in care, eligible individuals who have left care and children for whom a responsible adult cannot provide accommodation.  In fulfilling these duties, they must ensure that, when required, a child has somewhere safe to live while on bail, that meets the bail conditions.  For those children who are in care, this is often the child’s current placement e.g. foster care or residential home.  However, as this cohort of children and young people often have highly complex needs / situations this is not always possible, and the Trust will need to identify an alternative placement which meets the needs of the child.  For those children and young people not already in care, they may become looked after so that the relevant HSC Trust can provide them with suitable accommodation while on bail.   My Department, along with the Youth Justice Agency (YJA), is involved in several forums to discuss and highlight various issues, individual cases, tends and data analysis between children and young people and interaction with the juvenile justice system.  These include, for example;Children and Young People’s Strategic Partnership (CYPSP) Regional Offending Sub-Group, The Partnership Board for Woodlands Juvenile Justice Centre and Lakewood Regional Secure Care Centre,The Regional Practice Network for Unaccompanied Asylum-Seeking Children and,The Strategic Framework for Youth Justice Implementation Group In addition, as part of the Children’s Social Care Services Strategic Reform Programme established to address the challenges which led to the Independent Review of Children’s Social Care, there is a specific focus on enhancing capacity and capability of our residential care provision, in particular through ongoing work to develop a regional strategic plan for children’s residential care.  The Youth Justice Agency is a member of the overall Reform Programme Board and is also represented in the Residential Capacity Workstream which is taking forward this work.  Discussions regarding bail addresses have also taken place in the Fostering Workstream of the Reform Programme, in particular in relation to bail accommodation in a specialist foster placement. The existing bail fostering initiative which has been piloted in the Southern Trust area aims to provide short-term therapeutic foster placements for children remanded to Woodlands Juvenile Justice Centre who could not perfect bail due to lack of suitable accommodation being available.  The pilot has been relatively successful, a

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the accuracy of the My Waiting Times provision on the Health and Social Care Northern Ireland website, for each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The My Waiting Times webpage details the average waiting times for a patient’s first appointment with a clinician, having been referred by the GP. Patients are being managed based on clinical urgency and therefore waiting times can vary depending on each patient’s clinical condition. The average waiting times presented are for open referrals, and patients waiting within sub-specialties may experience a shorter waiting time or a significantly longer waiting time depending on the demand for a particular sub-speciality appointment.The methodology used for My Waiting Times NI ensures a comparative analysis and consistent approach to reporting waiting time information to patients and healthcare professionals, and is updated on a monthly basis, which is the same methodology used by NHS England. This information is sent to the Trusts to be verified prior to publication, and my Department is working with Trusts to improve the level of information provided to patients on waiting times.My Department has been working with NHS colleagues across the UK to provide further context to the average waiting time information which is available on the My Waiting Times NI webpage. My Department is currently exploring how the functionality can be enhanced to allow the publication of the top decile longest waiting times for assessments and treatments.When available, this additional data will provide further clarity to both patients and GPs in order to make more informed decisions about their referral pathway.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on a Racial Equality Strategy post-2025.
answer_text: We are pleased to have agreed a draft Framework for Race Relations and an initial two-year Delivery Plan and will soon be commencing a public consultation before bringing a final draft to the Executive for agreement. The Framework is built on the foundations laid by the previous Racial Equality Strategy and informed by engagement with a range of stakeholders.  The initial Delivery Plan outlines a range of interventions to target key issues, which is based on learning from the Public Health approach to other issues.Collectively, they seek to ensure departments, other agencies and key stakeholders are working together to tackle race relations issues so that we can create a more prosperous society for all.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any discussions her Department has had with the Department of Health, regarding the provision of appropriate accommodation for children who require a bail address.
answer_text: As the statutory duty to provide accommodation to a child in need sits with the Department of Health (DoH), my officials have ongoing engagement with Health colleagues to improve the availability of suitable accommodation in the community. 		The bailfostering “proof of concept” pilot, developed through a collaboration between the Southern HSC Trust and the Youth Justice Agency (YJA), is an example of the progress made to ensure that adequate, safe and appropriate accommodation is made available for children requiring a bail address within the Trust area.The pilot, funded by the Trust, has to date provided six successful bail fostering placements for children remanded to Woodlands who could not otherwise perfect bail. This success has meant that it has moved out of its pilot phase and is being delivered by the Southern HSC Trust as business as usual. There have been further discussions with DoH’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) on the potential to expand the scheme into the remaining Trusts.  However, as the resources required to place looked after children are already under pressure, this will have an impact on Trusts’ ability to offer the additional bail fostering placements.My Department and YJA continue to work closely with the Department of Health, SPPG and HSC Trusts through a range of different groups and fora. The Strategic Framework for Youth Justice Implementation Group is another example of this collaboration, where Health and Justice representatives work with a wider set of partners who share our aim to improve the way secure accommodation and allied services in the community are delivered to better support children, including those in contact with the justice system. My officials will continue to use these opportunities to consider options for children who cannot be safely returned home when given bail. I would, however, stress that it is important to recognise the challenges faced given the uncertainties around the numbers, circumstances and locations of children who may need to be accommodated, in conjunction with the resource pressures which all Departments are currently facing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has given consideration to extend Education Statements, which dictate that young people require support, beyond the age of 16 to the age of 18.
answer_text: The duties for the provision of education for a child with special educational needs (SEN) are set out in the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1996 Part II Article 3, which states that for the purposes of the provision of special education a ‘child’ is defined as ‘a person who attains the age of 19 years at any time during a school year at any school shall be deemed not to have attained that age until the day after the end of that school year.’ As part of the transition and annual review process, a statement of SEN may cease to be maintained once a young person leaves an education setting at either the end of year 12 or year 14.  For some children attending special schools and dependent on their birthday they may receive a year 15 at special school as they can remain in school while aged 19.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the awarding of Youth Capital Scheme funding to applicants who had previously received a letter of offer.
answer_text: A Letter of Offer is a binding agreement between the Department and the youth organisation. The Letter of Offer details the amount, eligibility period and conditions of funding, along with information on reporting requirements and any break clauses and termination information relating to the funding being offered.All youth schemes that applied for funding and received a letter of offer have had their works completed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department has taken to commission a service for people with pronounced autism beyond diagnosis in the (i) Northern Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) all other Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: (i) My Department recognises that autistic people may experience very different levels of support need, each shaped by their unique experiences and circumstances. Health and Social Care support for autistic people with complex needs, is determined by their specific presenting need. Where an individual has a diagnosed learning disability, they can receive support and service provision from Learning Disability services. Where complex needs relate to healthcare, physical disability or sensory impairment, Trusts’ Physical Disability Service can assess need and provide relevant supports such as equipment, social care or signposting.  Individuals presenting with Mental Health Needs may access support from Community Mental Health Teams. While Trusts apply ‘best fit’ principles to determine how individual needs can be most appropriately met, the Department is aware that it can be challenging to provide ongoing social care support where individuals do not meet the access thresholds for existing statutory services, including Learning Disability, Mental Health, or Physical & Sensory Disability Services.Within the Northern Health and Social Care Trust (NHSCT), autistic individuals can access a range of support post diagnosis depending upon assessed need as follows:Children and young people:The NHSCT Paediatric Autism Service provides pre-diagnostic telephone support, tailored post-diagnostic information and education programmes, post-diagnosis consultation for those up to age 18, and a range of individual and group interventions focusing on emotional regulation, neurodiversity awareness, and social skills. Digital resources such as podcasts, sensory videos and online programmes further supplement this support.Adults:The Adult ASD Service provides therapeutic interventions, including a post-diagnosis support group and short-term individual therapeutic input. It offers consultation and training to other Trust services and is also a lead partner of the multi-agency Northern Adult Autism Advisory service which provides monthly drop in advisory sessions held in different locations across the Northern Trust geography. For autistic adults who also have a learning disability, multi-disciplinary support is available from NHSCT Community Learning Disability Teams and depending upon an assessment of need, individuals can access a range of commissioned provision. This includes building-based day services and community-based day opportunities, supported living and residential placements and short break services. Autistic adults with a learning disability and complex needs may also access specialist services such as Learning Disability Clinical Psychology, Psychiatry, Positive Behaviour Support and inpatient Assessment and Treatment, alongside crisis supports. Direct Payments are also available to provide flexible support options.(ii) Each Trust provides post-diagnostic support according to local configuration, with services including therapeutic interventions, inform

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration he has given to adopting a person-centred approach, or best-fit model, when considering support for people with complex needs, as opposed to strictly adhering to criteria which defines a learning disability on the basis of IQ.
answer_text: I am aware that some people with complex needs, including autistic individuals, may experience challenges in accessing appropriate and timely support, particularly where they do not meet the diagnostic threshold for Adult Learning Disability services.Health and Social Care Trusts are required to assess individuals on the basis of their presenting needs and to consider which Programme of Care is best placed to meet those needs. While a confirmed diagnosis of learning disability is required to access Adult Learning Disability services, individual IQ level is not, in itself, a determinant of support, but rather one element used by clinicians alongside assessment of adaptive functioning and developmental history.I recognise that a cohort of young adults with learning difficulties, who are also autistic, may present with levels of functional and social vulnerability comparable to those with a learning disability, but may not clearly align with existing statutory Programmes of Care. This can create challenges in relation to continuity of care and clarity of pathways, particularly at transition to Adult Services, when they do not meet access thresholds for existing statutory support services such as Learning Disability, Mental Health or Physical & Sensory Disability Services.Trusts already apply professional discretion and ‘Best Fit’ principles when determining how individual needs may be met; however, current arrangements can lack regional consistency. As part of the delivery of the Autism Strategy 2023–2028, the Department, through the Strategic Planning and Performance Group, is reviewing the Adult Autism Care Pathway to improve consistency, clarify pathways and ensure alignment with NICE guidance.In parallel, the Department recently concluded a public consultation on the draft Learning Disability Service Model, which proposes a more person-centred, outcomes-focused approach to supporting adults with a learning disability. Issues relating to severe autism, access to services and the use of diagnostic criteria were raised during the consultation and are being considered as part of the analysis of responses which will be completed by early Spring 2026.Any future changes to care pathways, eligibility arrangements or service models will require formal policy consideration and will be subject to the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of children that have been held in the Woodlands Juvenile Justice Centre after bail has been granted, due to a lack of appropriate accommodation, in the last three years.
answer_text: Most children released on bail can return to their family home or their previous accommodation.  Where they are unable to do so, the relevant Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust has a statutory responsibility to secure suitable accommodation under the Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995. There are also occasions where the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) must approve the proposed bail address.Delays can occur in cases where a child is not previously known to Social Services, where they have multiple or complex needs which must be accommodated in a suitable environment or where the child cannot return to the family home due to risk(s) posed.  Figures across the last 3 years are as follows:2023 (January – December)63 instances of a delay between date bail granted and being perfected involving 50 children. In 56 of the 63 instances (46 children) the delay was in relation to lack of suitable accommodation:Awaiting an address approved by Social Services – 11 Approved address only available after a certain date – 2 Awaiting an address approved by Social Services & PSNI - 24 Awaiting an address approved by PSNI -19 Average delay: 14 days2024 (Jan – Dec)54 instances of a delay between date bail granted and perfected involving 41 children. In 48 of the 54 instances (38 young people) were delayed due to lack of suitable accommodation:Awaiting an address approved by Social Services – 15Awaiting a secure placement / Lakewood - 1 Awaiting an address approved by Social Services & PSNI - 17Awaiting an address approved by PSNI - 15Average delay: 12 days2025 (Jan- Dec)66 instances of a delay between bail being granted and being perfected involving 52 children. In 61 of the 66 instances (48 children) the delay was in relation to lack of suitable accommodation including:Awaiting an address approved by Social Services - 14 Awaiting an address approved by RQIA only - 1 Awaiting a secure placement/Lakewood - 2Awaiting an address approved by Social Services & PSNI - 21Awaiting an address approved by PSNI – 23Average delay: 12 days    Other reasons for a delay in perfecting bail included children refusing to sign their bail papers and preferring to remain in the JJC.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions he has taken to increase the provision of respite services for adults with additional needs, in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Northern HSC Trust report that Adult LD services currently are delivering all 14 commissioned short break beds. The Trust are working with a provider to develop an additional 2 short break beds in the Causeway area for people with learning disabilities and complex needs; this service is estimated to open in early 2027. The Trust recognises the challenges associated with the provision of respite to adults with learning disabilities and their families.The draft Learning Disability Service Model sets out a new approach to supporting adults with a diagnosed learning disability across Health and Social Care services. It outlines the outcomes we want to achieve, the principles that will guide delivery, and the actions required to drive improvement across the system. It has several key ambitions for adults with a learning disability with complex needs, which once delivered will support delivery of lifelong, co-ordinated support tailored to their needs and enabling choice and control.The Department of Health is leading on the reform of adult social care under the auspices of the Social Care Collaborative Reform Board. The Commissioning and Contracting workstream is taking forward the review of the regional care homes contract and specification which will include provision for respite/short break placements within the care home setting. My Department continues to operate within a constrained financial context and must prioritise work that can be delivered within current resources. Any future commissioning decisions will be informed by evidence of demand and a clear understanding of service need

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of existing accommodation options, available for children who require a bail address; and (ii) to detail any steps her Department is taking to increase accommodation capacity.
answer_text: The vast majority of children released on bail, whether granted by police or court, are able to return to their family home or their previous accommodation.  Where they are unable to do so, the relevant Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust has a statutory responsibility to secure suitable accommodation under the Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995.  On occasion, a child’s release on bail is delayed while efforts are made to secure appropriate accommodation in the community.  A small number of children are admitted to Woodlands Juvenile Justice Centre each year due to the lack of appropriate accommodation being immediately available.   Where this happens, Trusts work hard to provide accommodation within the shortest possible timeframe, but delays can occur in cases particularly where a child is not previously known to Social Services, or where they have multiple or complex needs which must be accommodated in a safe environment.  There are protocols in place to address issues around bail accommodation.  The Youth Justice Agency (YJA) will alert the relevant Trust at the earliest opportunity that a child will require accommodation if bail is granted.  Multi-agency meetings will be arranged to identify and agree suitable bail accommodation before release.  If the Trust cannot provide a suitable bail address, and therefore bail cannot be facilitated, then the Safeguarding Board NI “Resolution of Professional Differences Protocol” can be considered in line with the YJA Escalation Policy.  YJA staff ensure Court staff and Trusts understand the timelines for release so that accommodation decisions align with bail conditions and supervision arrangements.I recognise that there is a need to increase the provision of alternative accommodation in the community, to ensure children do not experience custody when their offending does not require it, but the statutory duty to provide accommodation to a child in need rests with the Department of Health.  That said, my officials continue to explore options with Health colleagues to address the issue.  One such example, is the YJA’s collaboration with the Southern HSC Trust on a bail fostering ‘proof of concept’ pilot.  The pilot aimed to provide short-term therapeutic foster placements for children remanded to Woodlands who could not perfect bail because they had no suitable home placement.  To date, there have been six successful bail fostering placements delivered through the scheme.  This success has meant that it has moved out of its pilot phase and is being delivered by the Trust as business as usual.  Discussions are ongoing with DoH’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) and Trusts on the potential to expand the scheme into other Trust areas.  My Department and the YJA are also engaged with the Department of Health, SPPG and HSC Trusts across a range of groups and fora with a common aim of improving outcomes for children, including those in the justice system.  They will continue to use these opp

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the total hours of Direct Payment support per week which has currently been approved to families of (i) children; (ii) young people; and (iii) elderly people, without access to a package of care, in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Northern Health and Social Care Trust’s Finance System is unable to retrieve information as to the number of hours of Direct Payment support approved for persons without access to a package of care. The information below details the total amount of hours per week the Northern Health and Social Care Trust paid in respect of direct payment:  Children (including Young People) 1838 Elderly (including Dementia)  5057  This information was taken from payments made in January 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline any engagement that has taken place between his Department, the Council for Catholic Maintained Schools and the Education Authority in relation to an Irish-medium unit at St John’s Primary School, Swatragh.
answer_text: Any significant change proposed to a school, including the establishment of an Irish-medium Unit, would require the publication of a statutory development proposal (DP).The Council for Catholic Maintained Schools (CCMS), as the planning and managing authority for Catholic Maintained schools, would have the responsibility for bringing forward a DP for the establishment of an Irish-medium Unit at St John’s Primary School, Swatragh.The Education Authority’s (EA) Operational Plan 2: 2024-26 includes an action in relation to the Swatragh, Maghera and Kilrea Area that CCMS ‘proposes to review maintained primary school provision in the area including the provision of Irish Medium provision in Swatragh’.My officials have liaised with CCMS, elected representatives, the Principal and Board of Governors of St John’s PS and Comhairle na Gaelscolaíochta to explain the steps in the DP process.  CCMS has established a Project Board, Project Steering Group and a Project Team to take forward the work stream from OP2 to look at the strategic development of Irish-medium education in the Swatragh, Maghera and Kilrea Area. Stakeholders from all relevant primary schools in these areas are participating in ongoing discussions which are progressing well.  It is envisaged that the Project’s work will be completed by the summer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the provision of domiciliary care packages in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust area.
answer_text: The Northern Trust has commenced a Homecare Reform programme to maximise capacity during the winter period and beyond. This is a multi-layered approach, with short, medium and long-term goals. In addition, the Trust continues to engage with the Department of Health in order to ensure regional consistency with service improvement and delivery. At the end of July this year the Northern Trust delivered 264,619 hours to 4,622 clients each week.  While there have been improvements in the position around unmet need in the Trust, reducing by 14% since July 2025, challenges still remain in securing services in some areas due to recruitment and retention in both the Independent and Statutory sectors. Independent Sector Providers have also cited the ongoing unresolved pay award as representing a significant challenge for them in their efforts to maintain stability within their services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of how the Direct Payments system is being implemented in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust region to support young adults with complex needs.
answer_text: The Direct Payments system is already established in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust and is provided as an option for service users, including young adults with complex needs within Self Directed Support provision.My Department has set a target for all Health and Social Care Trusts to improve the provision of Direct Payments within all programmes of care by 5% by March 2026. It is my assessment that the Northern Health and Social Care Trust are performing well with regards to this improvement initiative and have already exceeded this target.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the criteria used to determine how many hours per week of direct payment support a family with (i) a child; and (ii) a young adult, with complex needs is entitled to.
answer_text: Children Across all HSC Trusts in Northern Ireland – the Understanding The Needs of Children In Northern Ireland (UNOCINI) assessment framework is the standardised assessment model that is used within Children’s Services to capture an overview of assessed need for children and families. This framework affords consideration to assessed need in terms of support required. The UNOCINI assessment is structured around three core assessment domains;The child’s developmental needs Parental capacity Family and Environmental Factors(ii) Young AdultsThe Framework used to assess the needs of Young Adults is the Northern Ireland Single Assessment Tool (NISAT). The assessment domains that will be considered in arriving at a determination of the hours of support provided under Direct Payments for Young Adults are; Physical wellbeingMental and Emotional wellbeingPersonal relationshipsSocial and community inclusionIndependence and daily livingSafetyCarers supportHSC Trusts work in partnership with parents, carers and young adults in gathering the necessary information required to evaluate how identified needs may be best met. An assessment in respect of eligibility for Direct Payments is made by the relevant Health & Social Care Trust, as each assessment and identified areas of need are unique to the individual, it would not be possible to provide a generalised answer to the question being posed.  Where a requirement for a Direct Payment has been identified, the UNOCINI/ NISAT assessment would be presented to the Health and Social Care Trust’s Allocation/Resource Panel where approval would be sought for the number of hours assessed as being required.Direct Payments can be used for a variety of services which offer the child or young adult, new experiences and which promote greater levels of independence. These services may include short breaks, support to attend youth facilities or engage with activities and help with personal care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on her Department's actions to tackle delays in domestic violence cases going through the court system.
To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Independent Review of Invest NI.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail the progress made in ensuring that there is equality of access to (i) language appropriate curricular; and (ii) assessment material, for the Irish medium sector.
answer_text: The Department recognises the broader challenges faced by the Irish-Medium Education (IME) sector in accessing curriculum resources and suitable assessment arrangements in Irish. The Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland curriculum highlighted that currently, the curriculum is not meeting the diverse needs of all our pupils, including those in Irish-medium education. I am conscious that there may be the need for bespoke, tailored approaches for Irish-medium education.  As the new framework is developed, specific work on IME will be required to ensure the reformed curriculum meets the needs of all learners supporting equality of access for all pupils.As set out within TransformED, the Department is committed to investing in the further development of CCEA Computer Adaptive Assessment as a formative assessment tool at pupil level and to inform school self-evaluation. An Irish-medium CAA Numeracy assessment and a trans adaptation of the CAA English Numeracy test is currently in development. It is anticipated that this assessment will be operational across Key Stage 2 and 3 by autumn 2028. The Irish-medium adaptive Numeracy assessment was piloted with a sample of 158 pupils in 9 schools in January/February 2024 and again in May/June 2025 with 491 pupils across 14 schools. This assessment has been translated and adapted, including accommodations in line with international best practice thus ensuring that it does not disadvantage second-language pupils.Literacy assessments in Irish require a bespoke test. CCEA has worked in collaboration with schools and stakeholders to draw up an assessment framework for an Irish Literacy test which will now be integrated within the assessment tool infrastructure and test development, and piloting can then begin. From 2025-26 school year, interim assessments arrangements are being put in place to identify end of key stage pupil performance in literacy and numeracy via paper-based tests. All pupils in all Irish-medium schools in Years 4, 7 and 10 will participate in new Key Stage assessments during each year of the interim arrangements. Pupils in Irish medium schools will complete two tests at Key Stage 1 (Irish; and Numeracy) and three tests at Key Stage 2 and 3 (Irish, Literacy (English) and Numeracy) to reflect the introduction of English as a formal area of learning in Year 4. The tests will be provided by and marked by CCEA. Pupils will sit the written assessments in early March 2026. The Department will meet the costs of substitute cover for the invigilation of the assessment. CCEA will ensure robust development and quality assurance processes are in place. Irish Medium practitioners are currently drafting the tests. These teams will tailor the language within the tests to both the standard of the Levels of Progression, as well as the age-appropriate Irish vocabulary.  Three Irish literacy tests are being developed, for pupils in KS1 (Year 4), KS2 (Year 7) and KS3 (Year 10). Three numeracy tests are being deve

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether primary schools in the Magherafelt area will receive an increase to their shared education budget, within the 2025-26 school year.
answer_text: There are currently four primary schools in the Magherafelt area that access Mainstreamed Shared Education resource. It is not envisaged that schools will receive an increase to their Mainstreamed Shared Education budget within the 2025/26 school year. In relation to PEACEPLUS ASPIRE funding, there is an agreed rate of £126 per pupil completing 30 hours of Shared Education activity. There will not be an increase to this unit cost during the life of the project.My Department continues to make the case for increased funding to support Shared Education wherever possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how shared education funding has been allocated to primary schools in Mid Ulster, for the school year 2025-26.
answer_text: Mainstreamed Shared Education funding, administered by the Education Authority, has been notified to 34 primary schools in Mid Ulster (17 Partnerships) for the school year 2025/26 at a total of £170,960. Of those 34 schools, two are partnered with schools in the Causeway Coast and Glens. Allocations to schools are determined based on the assessment of Action Plans submitted, the application of Funding Models, and the level of demand across all Mainstreamed Shared Education Partnerships, within the limits of the available budget in the 2025/26 financial year. Allocations in respect of the period April-June 26 are subject to the availability of resources in the next financial year. In addition, the PEACEPLUS ASPIRE programme, led by the Education Authority, supports 29 primary schools in the Mid Ulster Constituency. Funding is allocated by application. Schools must apply to the PEACEPLUS ASPIRE programme and not be in receipt of Mainstreamed Shared Education funding.Funding is then allocated based on a unit cost. This is agreed at £126 per pupil completing 30 hours of Shared Education activity. Each school can apply for a maximum of 80 pupils.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the available support his Department has for school budgets when substitute cover is required due to a bereavement or other tragic circumstance.
answer_text: All grant aided schools are funded under the Common Funding Scheme (CFS).  The Common Funding Formula (CFF) is the Department’s mechanism for allocating budgets to schools funded through Local Management of Schools arrangements. The circumstances where the cost of substitute employees will be met from centre funds are detailed in paragraphs 6.5 to 6.8 of the CFS.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline any measures the Civil Service is undertaking to recruit more young people into its workforce.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail clarification of how temporary variations are granted to secondary schools.
answer_text: When a school is oversubscribed, it may apply to my department for additional places through the Temporary Variation (TV) process.Additional places can be approved where applicants have no alternative school places in the same sector within a reasonable travelling distance (defined as under one hour by public transport and no more than 15 miles), or where a sibling is already enrolled.  The recognised sectors are:Denominational (maintained and denominational grammar);Non-denominational (controlled and non-denominational grammar);Integrated; andIrish-medium (including Irish-medium units).Places are allocated strictly in line with the rank order as determined by each school’s admissions criteria, so approval of a TV does not guarantee admission for every child who meets the requirements.  My department may also approve ‘discretionary places’ in order to admit children further down the rank order where necessary.The full process is set out here on my department’s website.A review of the TV policy is currently underway, with consultation open until 3 November.A separate TV policy applies for post-16 admissions.  While based on the same principles, it also takes account of pupils’ preferred subject choices and whether these can be provided in another school of the same sector in the area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will present the Sexual Orientation Strategy to the Executive.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the appointment of the language commissioners under the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans his Department has to support sports clubs with capital investment.
