answer_text: You will be aware that in May 2024, I launched the Northern Ireland Football Fund, and my officials are currently evaluating applications from performance clubs. Additionally, they are finalising proposals to address the significant shortfall in grassroots facilities, including pitches and changing rooms.In 2024/25, I allocated funding for the Your School Your Club initiative, enabling schools to establish partnerships with sports clubs so that school sports facilities can be upgraded and used outside school hours, to help meet the growing demand for sporting and recreational spaces across Northern Ireland. I am committed to continuing this initiative and have allocated £3m of funding for 2025/26, to be delivered through Sport NI, my Department’s arm’s-length body.I have also directed my officials to explore the feasibility of additional programs, including potential Olympic Legacy Funding, inspired by the success of our athletes at the Paris 2024 Olympics, should resources become available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on forthcoming capital grants to be delivered through Sport NI.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Sport NI.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Following confirmation of capital budget Sport NI are in the process of developing programmes to deliver capital infrastructure projects aligning to the objectives of Active Living - Sport and Physical Activity Strategy for Northern Ireland. “Sport NI are currently developing a Safety Management Programme for sport, as well as a five year Your School Your Club (YSYC) programme. YSYC is an initiative to help better utilise the school estate for sport and physical activity by developing partnerships to provide new or upgrade community access to school facilities, creating or facilitating additional capacity for the sport and community sector.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact Sport NI directly.  Making direct contact with Sport NI will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an outline of actions taken by his Department to replace the LEADER programme which had previously benefited rural communities.
answer_text: As Minister, I acknowledge the closure of the LEADER programme continues to be of concern to our rural communities and stakeholders.My officials have commenced an intensive policy development programme using a co-design process with a wide range of stakeholders to develop future rural policy for Northern Ireland following the UK’s exit from the EU and the closure of the NI Rural Development Programme (2014-2020), through which the LEADER initiative was funded. I am keen that this process reflects the diverse nature of our rural communities in 2025.As all departments are responsible for exercising their functions both in urban areas and in rural areas, my Department is engaging with other government departments to explore future opportunities for supporting rural communities and to help identify how DAERA can work more effectively with them in the future to help deliver better outcomes for our rural people and places.All of my Executive colleagues have agreed the support and participation of their departments in this co-design process, and it is my intention that draft proposals for a future rural policy will be consulted on later this year.While this important policy development work is being undertaken, my Department will continue to support rural communities on the ground, working in partnership with SEUPB and local councils to take forward Executive priorities on PEACEPLUS, City and Growth, and Complementary Fund initiatives. Alongside this we will also continue to deliver initiatives under the Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation Framework.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the impact of the trade tariffs announced by the President of the United States of America and the British Prime Minister on 8 May 2025.
answer_text: I have been clear that the current situation is deeply regrettable and has created much uncertainty and concern for businesses, workers and their families. I am monitoring the impact of US tariffs and any countermeasures closely, primarily through my Tariffs Working Group which meets on a regular basis and provides me with sector-level insights and views on the wider economic impact. I am also engaging regularly with both the British and Irish Government’s to share developments and outline the case for consideration of our unique, all-island trading profile. As to the joint British-US announcement on May 8, my officials are taking the time to work through what it means for the north – and I am pressing the British Government for more details in that regard.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions his Department has taken to increase the capacity for residential breaks for (i) children; and (ii) young adults,  who live with complex needs, within the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: (i) My Department’s strategic response to the increasing level of need for Children with Disability across Northern Ireland has been set out through a regional Framework for Children with Disability. This has been partly progressed through the Regional Children’s Services Strategic Reform Programme, which my Department is leading on, and which was established in response to Professor Ray Jones’ Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services. The regional needs of Children with Disability are being considered across specific workstreams focusing on Residential Care (Workstream 3), Fostering (Workstream 4), Children with Disability (Workstream 5), and Family Support (Workstream 9). Focused Work in respect of the projected residential care need for Children with Disability across the five HSC Trusts, in addition to specific work regarding enhancing the Short Break provision (both residential & fostering) for Children with Disability across each of the five HSC Trusts is being completed through the Regional Social Care Reform Workstreams. This work remains ongoing, and it will form the basis for future commissioning of services and investment plans in the coming years. Within the Reform Programme, we are looking carefully at the models of residential care, short breaks and fostering for children which are working well, to see how they could be replicated and expanded across Northern Ireland. The Strategic Reform Programme aims to ensure that we have the right services to meet the holistic needs of young people (including those with disability), now and in the future.On 23.10.2024, I had approved additional regional funding for the Children with Disability service of up to £13m a year in multi-year funding, including up to £2m in year funding. It is intended that the additional funding will assist regionally with enhancing Short Breaks, improving Residential Capacity and expanding Family, Therapeutic and Behavioural Supports in the community.My Department has worked with the Northern Health and Social Care Trust and RQIA to agree a variation to the legislation to plan for two 17-year olds to move from one of the Trust’s long-term residential homes. The Northern HSC Trust continue to work with a voluntary/community sector provider to plan the transition. The transition of the young people will allow the children residing in a Trust Short Break facility to transfer to the longer-term residential home, and subsequently for the Short Break facility to reopen short breaks.(ii) The Northern HSC Trust are currently delivering its full commissioned provision of Short Breaks for adults with a Learning Disability. The Northern HSC Trust provide Short Breaks in two statutory Short Break homes for up to 6 adults with Learning Disability in each unit.  The Northern HSC Trust also block book 2 beds in an independent provider Nursing home.  In addition, the Northern HSC Trust are engaging with other providers to try to increase the number of beds availabl

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide an update on the addition of the Dexcom G6 device to the Drug Tariff, in order that it can be accessed by patients.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland, continuous glucose monitors currently listed on the NI Drug Tariff can be provided to patients, with the most appropriate system determined in consultation between the clinician and the patient and/or the patient’s carer.  Dexcom G6 sensor is a type of complex Continuous Glucose monitoring device that is not currently listed on the NI Drug tariff.  Some patients have been self-funding these devices as they enable closed loop technology, although it may not be the most appropriate system for some patients.The Department of Health is unable to add devices to the NI Drug Tariff. The application process for medical devices to be added to the NI Drug Tariff has to be completed by the supplier, through the appropriate channels.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the next steps in relation to the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the review of Together: Building a United Community.
answer_text: The T:BUC Strategy reflects the Executive’s commitment to improving community relations here between people from different political, ethnic and religious backgrounds.There has been considerable progress made to-date and T:BUC has had a positive impact on the lives of many people.  It has brought people together through sport and education, and through T:BUC camps, and it has removed interface barriers. We have also delivered the Urban Villages initiative and the development of Shared Neighbourhoods and progress continues to be made. We are currently considering recommendations from the T:BUC Review and these will shape the timeline for the development of a refreshed approach to Good Relations.In the meantime, the delivery of T:BUC continues to positively impact those most in need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will consider changing legislation relating to traffic movement around the dropping off and picking up of school children at bus stops and drop-off points in light of recent tragedies.
answer_text: I am committed to considering any initiatives that can improve road safety, which is a priority for me as Minister.  I have asked my officials to look at all the options that may be available to improve bus safety for school children and to report back to me as a matter of urgency; following which I will decide on next steps. I have also asked my officials to reinforce the messaging to help keep all road users safe, especially the vulnerable.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the recruitment of Commissioners as provided for by the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022.

To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what support his Department has offered to Belfast City Council and Ards Comhaltas Ceoltóirí Éireann in their efforts to bring the Fleadh Cheoil na hÉireann to Belfast.
answer_text: Levelling up our cultural agenda and promoting the rich cultural heritage of what Northern Ireland has to offer is one of my key priorities.  My Department is committed to help everyone within our communities to live positive, fulfilled lives surrounded by diverse opportunities to work, enjoy themselves and get involved.Hosting the 2026 All Ireland Fleadh complements my priorities very well.  I understand the value of participating in the arts and the personal rewards that can bring along with the significant collective social and economic benefits.  In July 2024, my Permanent Secretary provided a letter of support on behalf of my Department as the endeavours to bring the Fleadh were underway. Bringing a celebration of music, language, song and dance to the city and beyond is to be very much welcomed and will complement the UNESCO City of Music status gained by Belfast in November 2021.Belfast City Council is one of the public bodies who can access the services of my Department’s Translation Hub; and the Arts Council, an arms length body of my Department, would be pleased to assist with advice and input as plans develop.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the regulations in relation to tree cutting on farmers’ land where power lines are likely to be impacted should trees fall.
answer_text: Whilst it is a requirement of the Cross-Compliance Verifiable Standards that farmers must not remove landscape features except by prior written permission from the Department, there are some exceptions to this.Hedgerow trees, which are considered a landscape feature, may be removed if there are health and safety reasons, unless a tree preservation order is in place.  In other words, if this activity is necessary for health and safety reasons which would include the scenario illustrated, a Cross-Compliance breach will not be attributed to that farmer.Furthermore, where the trees are within a woodland of 0.2 hectares or more, felling of these trees is regulated under the Forestry Act (Northern Ireland) 2010. In normal circumstances felling these trees requires a felling licence.  However, the Act includes a number of exemptions, one of which applies to tree felling carried out by, or at the request of, an electricity undertaker, because the tree is or will be in such close proximity to an electricity line.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any transport provision that exists for people in residential homes to attend a medical appointment.
answer_text: Where an individual resides in a registered residential care home it is important that appropriate arrangements are in place to facilitate the attendance at personal appointments including medical appointments.Northern Ireland Ambulance Service provides non-emergency patient transport services which enables individuals to get to routine appointments at hospitals and treatment centres throughout Northern Ireland. The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service provides transport for patients:Attending outpatient appointments at hospitalRequiring investigation or treatment at hospitalBeing admitted to hospitalBeing transferred between hospitalsBeing discharged from hospital.In certain circumstances, individuals may have a relative or friend who will provide transport to and from personal commitments. Registered residential home providers may wish to operate a transport service and charge service users who wish to make use of communal vehicles to attend personal appointments. In operating a transport scheme, the registered provider must ensure that all vehicles are road worthy with up-to-date road tax, vehicle registration and current MOT certificates where appropriate and that drivers and passengers are properly insured against loss, damage and for personal injury.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will consider extending the deadline for training for the Soil Nutrient Health Scheme in light of the widespread power outages which are affecting thousands of rural dwellers, many of whom are farmers.
answer_text: The disruption caused by power outages in the recent storm has been significant for many people, however I can confirm that there is currently no fixed deadline for farmers to complete their Soil Nutrient Health Scheme (SNHS) training. Therefore, I am pleased to advise that this disruption will not disadvantage anyone who has received their soil analysis results and is ready to complete the training.  Nevertheless, I would encourage farmers to complete their training as soon as practical to allow them to take a planned approach to nutrient applications to benefit their business and protect the environment.The College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise website provides details of ways to access the training (CAFRE) SNHS training at https://www.cafre.ac.uk/business-support/agriculture/environment/soil-nutrient-health-scheme/
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action his Department has taken to improve postnatal mental health services for both mothers and fathers suffering with postnatal depression and anxiety.
answer_text: It is recognised that the postnatal period may be a time of considerable psychological and social stress for both mothers and fathers and this may contribute to depressive symptoms developing.The last two years have seen the roll-out of the specialist perinatal mental health teams across the five Trusts. These teams provide a comprehensive service to women with a wide range of mental health problems in both the antenatal and postnatal periods, including illnesses such as postnatal depression and postnatal psychosis.  While this service is for mothers, our teams can offer support and psychoeducation to their partners and direct them to appropriate services to seek help.As well as these specialist services there are a wide range of services in maternity, primary care and the community and voluntary sector to help women with postnatal depression.There are a range of services available for men within general mental health services and within the community and voluntary sector. As Community Perinatal Mental Health teams grow and evolve, it may be possible that they are able to offer more by the way of support and intervention to the partners of the mothers they are seeing.In addition, my officials are also progressing the provision of a Mother and Baby Unit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has reconsidered the suggestion to introduce post-birth questionnaires for all new fathers in all hospitals, as advocated for by the group Dad’s Voice.
answer_text: My officials have advised that there are currently no plans to introduce a specific post-birth questionnaire for new fathers as there is currently a process in place to capture feedback from mothers and fathers.All mothers, fathers and families are currently encouraged to use the Care Opinion platform to share their experiences of health and social care across Northern Ireland.Care opinion is an important platform to ensure peoples voices are heard at all levels of our system.  Care Opinion supports people to tell us what really matters to them, in their own words and in their own time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has any plans to announce a call for new builds for primary schools.
answer_text: Due to the many existing and emerging pressures on an already constrained capital budget, I have no plans to announce a call for new major capital projects at this time.I will continue to make the case for significantly increased and sustained investment in our schools’ estate, however until I receive additional funding, difficult decisions will continue to have to be taken.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on how he plans to address the significant issues faced by staff and students at Holy Family Primary School, Magherafelt, as a result of their outdated school building, which includes damp and mould.
answer_text: Education Authority (EA) Maintenance officers have been engaging with the school and contractors to scope additional roof repairs required to improve the ongoing issues with damp throughout the school. Maintenance will review the recommendations from specialists and complete any critical works required. The internal finishes will be made good once the roofing repairs are completed. Programmes will be agreed with the school and contractors before work commences on site.EA Maintenance has also reported that structural remedial work is required in an area of brickwork. The work recommended by the structural engineer is currently being programmed with contractors. A structural engineer’s survey on a mobile has recommended that, while the two classrooms are safe to use in the short-term, replacement would be required within 12 months. The Department has initiated an Unavoidable Minor Works project to replace the mobile and EA Maintenance will ensure it is safe to use until it is replaced.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has had any correspondence with the Drinking Water Inspectorate in relation to their thresholds for determining acceptable drinking water in relation to taste and smell.
answer_text: I have had no correspondence with the Drinking Water Inspectorate regarding the threshold for determining the acceptability of drinking water in relation to taste and smell.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he has taken to recruit staff to domiciliary care teams in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: One of my officials has been in contact with the NHSCT and they have provided this update: ‘NHSCT continue to work with Regional Shared Services Team in an ongoing rolling recruitment programme for Homecare Staff across all localities, with a total of 12 recruitment exercises completed since April 2024 to date.   This resulted in 37 new staff members who chose contracted hours.   In addition to staff members with contracted hours, 71 new staff members chose to avail of a Bank contract within the Homecare service.   However, this has been offset by 65 staff members with contracted hours who left the service since April 2024.   Off the 65 leavers, 29 were due to ill health or retirement, which reflects the challenges associated with the physically demanding nature of this role against an aging workforce within this staff group.Recruitment and retention in the Homecare workforce continues to be very challenging and remains an area of focus for the Trust. Despite ongoing efforts, the NHSCT continue to have a high number of Homecare Worker vacancies.’

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has given any consideration to amending the closed season for the spreading of slurry, in order to take account of variations in weather.
answer_text: My Department has given careful consideration to the suggestion to regulate slurry spreading based solely on weather conditions rather than fixed dates.The dates of the closed period are based on local data and scientific research by the Agri Food and Biosciences Institute (AFBI). DAERA commissioned AFBI to undertake a comprehensive review of the underpinning science in 2020 and again in 2024. The reviews concluded that there is no scientific evidence nor agronomic need to support amending the closed period. The closed spreading period for slurry is a key requirement of the Nutrients Action Programme (NAP) Regulations. It represents a time when the risk of nutrient run off is high and there is minimal nutrient uptake because grass/crop growth is limited by short day length and lower temperatures.  The AFBI analysis concluded that “The current evidence base supports the closed period as the most effective strategy for minimising the risks associated with slurry spreading from 15 October to the 31 January.  Removal of the closed period and reliance solely on the current NAP restrictions on weather and soil conditions, (i.e. restrict applications based on soil moisture and rainfall conditions), will not sufficiently mitigate the risk associated with slurry applications during this period.”Ultimately, the aim is to protect our watercourses from pollution at a time when major improvements in water quality are so badly needed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 18853/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions he has taken to support those suffering from dementia and cannot live independently as a result.
answer_text: With an ageing population, the demand on social care services for older people has increased significantly.  A referral to Social Services for an assessment of need is required in order to enable people to have access to support services through the Health and Social Care Trusts.  In addition, all HSC Trusts offer Carer’s Assessments which will identify what support may be required for the carer.  Through these assessment processes, there are alternative options which are considered to develop a personalised support plan which will enable people to remain living safely in their own homes for as long as they wish e.g. direct payments, cash grants, domiciliary care, Shared Lives services with a placement in a care home also being an option, depending on the individuals assessed need. Social Services will also work with service users and their family/carer to explore informal supports available before exploring the need for statutory services, this includes people with dementia who have been assessed as requiring support to remain at home or requiring a placement.  My department has been leading change initiatives to support the adult social care reform programme.  My department has established a Social Care Collaborative Forum (the Collaborative Forum) to provide vision, along with strategic advice and guidance on social care, and take forward agreed actions to support improvement and transformation.  Workstreams have been established to deliver short, medium, and longer-term actions.  Workstreams are: Maximising Capacity; Workforce; Enhancing Care in Care Homes; Data, Research and Evidence; Communications; Commissioning and Contracting; Supporting Unpaid Carers; and Supported Living. 		In addition, there has been a specific focus on dementia which is led through the Regional Dementia Project Board which has been established to lead improvement of dementia services across the region.  A Social Care Lead, with responsibility for dementia, liaises with all HSC trusts and other key stakeholders to ensure that there is a focus on people with dementia, including those who require additional support to safely remain at home and those who require a placement in a care home.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Tourism Action Plan.

To ask the Minister of Health whether there has been a reduction in the number of people in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust awaiting a full or partial package of care since 31 October 2024, when the figure was 1428.
answer_text: There has been a reduction in the number of people in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust awaiting full and part packages of care since 31st October 2024.  At the end of November this figure was 1475 people and then this dropped by 91 to 1384 at the end of December 2024.  This is a positive result when we measure this against an increase in new referrals for packages of care and the Winter period when demand increases.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the options available to those who require around the clock care in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) are responsible for the planning and delivery of services, aligned to an individual’s assessed needs.Following an assessment of need, the Northern HSC Trust’s named worker can use a range of options to meet the critical need of an individual. For an individual who requires 24-hour care in their own home the options include a bespoke package of care and self-directed support. 24-hour care options outside of the service users own home include supported living, residential and nursing care. The Trust named worker also has short-term 24-hour options such as Short Break Services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether there will be an increased budget allocation for road resurfacing schemes before the 2025-26 financial year; and (ii) to detail when the proposed resurfacing of Draperstown Main Street, at the roundabout and B47 junction with B40 and B41, take place.
answer_text: The additional funding allocated to my Department through the October Monitoring Round includes almost £19m towards essential and structural maintenance of our roads and street lighting work to progress existing and new schemes.Work has started on the planning and delivery of new schemes for delivery this financial year. However, the development of the programmes is on-going so full breakdown of these is not yet available. While many roads including Main Street, Draperstown would undoubtedly benefit from investment, due to a limited budget, resurfacing work must be taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need of intervention. Full details of the schemes to be taken forward will be published in my Department’s report to Mid Ulster Council.In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there are any plans to increase the gritting capacity in the Western Division of the network, specifically in terms of the number of gritters currently operational within the Magherafelt depot.
answer_text: In accordance with the current Winter Service policy, my Department generally salts roads that carry more than 1,500 vehicles per day and, in exceptional circumstances, roads with difficult topography and carrying between 1,000 and 1,500 vehicles per day.The application of this policy ensures an equitable service across Northern Ireland and ensures that 28% of the total road network, which carries around 80% of traffic, is salted, at an annual cost of approximately £6 million.Currently the gritter fleet operating out of our Magherafelt yard has sufficient capacity to deliver our Winter Service policy as outlined above and there are no plans to increase this at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action he has taken to increase the provision of respite services for adults with additional needs in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on his policy proposals for credit unions.
answer_text: Question 11 – AQO 1223/22-27The working group I established following the launch of the Irish League of Credit Unions policy manifesto is engaging with Treasury and the Financial Conduct and Prudential Regulatory Authorities.The working group is a partnership between my Department, the Irish League of Credit Unions and the Ulster Federation and, through co-design, it is examining the regulatory, legislative and policy requirements needed to deliver the modernisation that will allow the Credit Union movement in the north to flourish.  I intend to deliver these changes within the current mandate, and I am pleased to announce that I will bring finalised policy proposals to consultation early next year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of how effective direct payments hours are in terms of offering relief to family carers.
answer_text: A Direct Payment, which a Health and Social Care Trust has agreed to fund, must be used to purchase services in accordance with assessed need. The Department of Health do feel that direct payment hours are effective in offering relief to family/carers.  Their individualised approach ensures that care packages are tailored to each person’s need.  Direct Payments are utilised across a number of Programmes of Care within Health and Social Care.  Within Direct Payments there is the process of the Direct Payment recipient managing the budget and becoming an employer.   To assist in this process the Department of Health has commissioned a regional service which provide an Independent Information, Advice, and Advocacy Service to Support Independent Living.  The Centre for Independent Living (CILNI) provide the service currently to Service Users and Carers in all Programmes of Care in Northern Ireland.   The Service includes: helping Direct Payment recipients understand their role as an employer; facilitating access to a range of accredited insurance and Payroll Services and liaising with Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts to explore the range of options available to Service Users and Carers. The Department of Health recognises the crucial role carers play in supporting their family/carer, helping them remain independent and in their local community.  The Department of Health and HSC Trusts remain committed to ensuring Service Users and Carers receive appropriate service provision support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people currently awaiting a full or partial package of care in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The number of people currently awaiting a domiciliary care package in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust is listed in the tables below.   This has been separated into those awaiting full packages of care and those awaiting part packages of care and data is in relation to position at 31st October 2024:Full Packages of Care: TRUSTINDIVIDUALS WAITING NHSCT918Part Packages of Care:TRUSTINDIVIDUALS WAITING NHSCT510My department closely monitor all five HSC Trusts’ waiting lists for domiciliary care and regularly meet with Trusts to understand their waiting list pressures.  Northern Health and Social Care Trust continues to have the highest number of people awaiting a full or part package of care.  The level of unmet need has remained at this level for the past number of months.  Northern Trust has the highest elderly population and the elderly would be the biggest service user group that are in receipt of packages of care. Trusts have indicated that their ‘new referral’ rates are rising therefore making it more difficult to reduce the unmet need list.The Northern Trust are currently running a pilot to increase domiciliary care capacity in the rural areas which involves paying a higher rate for rural calls and also enables the Trust to maximise capacity within the independent sector provider rotas that are taking part in this pilot.  All Trusts continue to prioritise hospital discharges.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the actions his Department has taken to increase the recruitment of domiciliary care assistants in 2024.
answer_text: My officials continue to develop and implement a range of initiatives to increase the recruitment of domiciliary care staff.  These include:Developing the Social Care Workforce Strategy 2025-2035.  This strategy, which encompasses domiciliary care staff, is fundamental to recognising and supporting the social care workforce.  The strategy sets out priorities and actions on how we will attract, recruit, grow, retain and develop the workforce.  It is envisaged that this will raise the profile and recognition of all social care workers as a skilled and competent workforce thereby encouraging and supporting recruitment.   The Strategy will be launched on 12th December 2024.In partnership with the Northern Ireland Social Care Council, work is ongoing to develop the training, career and qualification pathways for the social care workforce.  This includes introducing the Level 2 Certificate in Safe and Effective Practice which provide domiciliary care staff with accredited training in the essential skills and values needed for social care work.  Launched in September 2024, the Department has supported its uptake via non-recurrent training funding to private domiciliary care providers.  Additionally, we have funded mentoring and coaching programs to assist employers and individuals participating in the training.   In partnership with all stakeholders’ social care campaigns are ongoing to raise the profile and recognise the significant contribution that the domiciliary care workforce provides.  The Social Care Fair Work Forum has also been established which will embed Fair Work initiatives and improve the terms and conditions for domiciliary care workers employed across all parts of the social care sector. Significant work is ongoing to build the case for the additional funding that will be required to deliver this.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to confirm that the promotion of regional balance will be a core component of the Programme for Government 2024-2027.
answer_text: Throughout the draft Programme for Government a commitment to regional balance can be seen by all departments, for example, in the Grow a Globally Competitive and Sustainable Economy, and the Building New Foundations sections of the document. The Wellbeing Dashboard also provides geographic breakdowns to make sure that inequalities that exist across different areas, including areas of multiple deprivation, are clear for all to see. This will allow us to see where regional inequalities are and how these can be addressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the economic opportunities of biomethane.
To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the review of the Home to School Transport policy.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on their plans to develop a Racial Equality Strategy post 2025.
answer_text: It is a key priority to fully deliver the commitments in the current Racial Equality Strategy, it is also important that post 2025 there is a clear mechanism and overarching framework for achieving racial equality and eradicating racism in our society. An initial workshop took place on 11 September 2024 with the Racial Equality Subgroup and the Travellers and Roma Thematic Groups, to gather initial thoughts on the way forward. Feedback from this workshop, along with input gathered through a process of collaboration with key stakeholders, including the wider minority ethnic sector, will inform any future strategic approach. We remain committed to ending racism and making this a fair and inclusive society where everyone is made to feel welcome and safe.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what is the percentage of farmers who have successfully entered the Soil Nutrient Health Scheme before the registration deadline closed, broken down by zone.
answer_text: I am pleased with the response to the Soil Nutrient Health Scheme since it first opened in 2022. The overall percentage of farm businesses registered for the Soil Nutrient Health Scheme to date is 91.07% (18,661 farms), against a target of 75%. This breaks down into the following percentages for each zone:30.03% in Zone 1 (Southeast)34.14% in Zone 2 (Southwest)26.90% in Zone 3 (Northwest)Zone 1 of the Soil Nutrient Health Scheme opened for registrations between June and September 2022.  Registrations for Zone 2 were invited in summer 2023, followed by Zone 3 in summer this year, with registration closing on 6 September 2024.  Farmers in Zones 1 and 2 who had not registered in their respective years, had the opportunity to apply in subsequent years.  Some farm businesses have fields in more than one zone, and the percentages I have provided are based on the first year a farm business registered for the scheme. I plan to open Zone 4 for registrations in 2025 and I encourage farm businesses, in all zones, which have not yet applied, to register for the scheme next year as participation in the scheme is a condition for receipt of the new Farm Sustainability Payment.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the review of the Home to School Transport policy.
answer_text: A review of the Home to School Transport Policy commenced in 2018, however this was paused by the then Minister due to the pandemic and further suspended pending the outcome of The Independent Review of Education. It is important to note that the transport policy supports other policies within the Department, such as parental preference and Special Educational Needs (SEN). Work is progressing on an end-to-end review of SEN and work is also underway on an action plan arising from the EA Landscape Review, to include detailed consideration of the operational delivery of services such as school transport. I have also recently received advice on the Independent Review of Education from officials and I will announce a response to the recommendations in the coming weeks. Any review of the Home to School Transport Policy should complement and build on the outcomes from these workstreams and it is in this context that I will consider the utility and timing of a new separate review of the transport policy. Given the very significant budgetary pressures facing my Department I will also need to consider the affordability of any changes to the transport policy that would result in the widening of the criteria to access transport provision. The cost of delivering the current school transport service for over 90,000 pupils is significant at over £120 million in 2023/24. Any proposals for policy change would need to be carefully scoped out and costed within the context of a detailed policy review and would likely require changes to legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of recorded suicides in each of the last ten years, broken down by (i) gender; and (ii) Health and Social Care Trust area.
answer_text: Data on the numbers of deaths from suicide up to 2022, broken down by gender, and Health and Social Care Trust can be found in tables 1 and 8 respectively. Provisional headline totals for suicide deaths registered in 2023 and quarter 1 2024 are also available from table 1c of the Registrar General Quarterly Tables. Final figures for 2023 are not yet available but headline totals will be published as part of the Registrar General Annual Report, currently scheduled for late November 2024. The Registrar General Quarterly Tables for quarter 2 2024 are due to be published on Tuesday 24 September 2024.There are some situations where the registration of the death can be delayed, specifically where the death has been accidental, unexpected, or suspicious, and must be referred to the Coroners Service for Northern Ireland (CSNI), only to be registered after completion of the investigation. It should be noted that suicides registered during a particular time will therefore not be reflective of suicides that occurred in that period. In 2019 NISRA and the CSNI commenced a review of suicide statistics following the identification of a classification issue in published statistics which concluded in 2022. This marked a major revision to this important statistical series with a break in the time series being introduced from 2015. Comparisons with data prior to 2015 should therefore be made with caution.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on (i) the 2024-25 budget provided to the Affordable Warmth Scheme; and (ii) how he will ensure that those accepted onto the scheme are able to have the work carried out as soon as possible.
answer_text: I allocated an opening budget of £7.175m for the Affordable Warmth Scheme (AWS) to cover £5.2m of approvals carried forward and £1.975m for new approvals. The Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE) is issuing grant approvals monthly within the funding allocated. When a customer receives written approval from the NIHE to complete measures under the AWS, the customer has 3 months to complete the approved works. It is the responsibility of the customer to source and appoint their own contractor to complete the works. Extensions can be provided in circumstances where applicants are finding it difficult to appoint a suitable contractor or complete the works.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide an update on the progress to appoint Commissioners under the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022.
answer_text: We are committed to establishing the three new bodies outline in the Act, and we were pleased to announce that the competitions launched on 10th March 2025 and closed on Friday 28 March 2025. It is anticipated that interviews will take place in June / July and, following this, appointments will be made.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of applications accepted by the Affordable Warmth Scheme; and (ii) the amount funding available for the scheme during the 2024-25 financial year, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Statistical information for the Affordable Warmth Scheme (AWS) is classed as an official/national statistic.  This is currently officially released through my Department’s annual Housing Statistics Report and its quarterly Housing Bulletin (at Housing statistics | Department for  Communities (communities-ni.gov.uk) https://datavis.nisra.gov.uk/communities/northern-ireland-housing-bulletin-april-june-2024.html) to allow a more timely release. The Code of Practice for the publication of national/official statistics means my Department can only share information for Quarter 1 of 2024-25 that has been published already within the quarterly Housing Bulletin. Between April 2024 and June 2024, 333 Affordable Warmth grant applications were formally approved by the Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE). Within the same period, 813 new Affordable Warmth grant applications were received by the NIHE for consideration under the scheme. I allocated an opening budget of £7.175m for the AWS to cover £5.2m of approvals carried forward and £1.975m for new approvals. Accordingly, Affordable Warmth approvals are being prioritised within the available envelope of funding. Affordable Warmth funding is not allocated at Local Council / constituency level but prioritises applications with no heating and that require priority 1 (insulation and draught proofing) and priority 2 (heating) measures.  The number of applications approved in a month is split across all Council areas that have applications with priority 1 and 2 measures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he intends to review how SureStart operates in terms of geographical wards, given old wards are being used as a mechanism for identifying those entitled to access their services.
answer_text: A recent independent review of Sure Start determined that the current arrangements for targeting support remain the most appropriate.  These use the latest official measure of deprivation in Northern Ireland (the Northern Ireland Multiple Deprivation Measure (NIMDM)) to identify the most disadvantaged geographical areas. The review found that around 48% of children aged 0-3 have access to Sure Start services, which are available in (at least) the 25% most disadvantaged areas in Northern Ireland.  I recently brought 22 areas into Sure Start on a permanent basis and agreed continued provision to a further 153 legacy areas, to secure this coverage.Managers of the 38 Sure Start projects also have discretion to provide services to families outside the above catchment areas, subject to identified need and resource availability.Further expansion of Sure Start will be considered within the Executive’s Early Learning and Childcare Strategy and will be dependent on funding and workforce availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline when she expects to publish the findings of the consultations on the Non-domestic and Domestic rating measures.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the training that social workers working in Health and Social Care Trusts receive in relation to coercive control.
answer_text: Social Workers employed in Health and Social care Trusts in Northern Ireland are encouraged to complete the Regional Domestic Abuse Context and Change Modules through LearnHSCNI which includes specific learning on Coercive Control. In addition, each Trust offer specific training on Domestic Abuse and Coercive Control. Domestic Abuse and coercive control are also included in other training programmes including mandatory training programmes such as Safeguarding children and adults.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the extent to which (i) magistrates; and (ii) other criminal justice system employees are trained in relation to issues around coercive control.
answer_text: I consider it essential that frontline professionals have an appropriate awareness and understanding of domestic abuse in all its forms, including coercive control, and how to recognise it.  This applies across the criminal justice system as a whole, as well as more widely, in terms of those who have a role to play in ensuring that offenders are brought to justice and that we can better support victims through the criminal justice system.  As you will be aware, the judiciary are independent, including with respect to training. I am bound to respect that independence. The Lady Chief Justice has statutory responsibility for the provision of training for the independent judiciary.  She has recently publicly restated her commitment to ensuring that the judiciary receive appropriate training, and I welcome this ongoing commitment. Whilst delivery of such training is a matter for the Judicial Studies Board, which is independent of the Department of Justice, we are committed to sharing training and awareness-raising resources that have been developed by and for our justice partners, for information, with the Board, where that is appropriate and helpful.My Department is required to publish an annual training statement, setting out information about the level of training undertaken by staff in criminal justice system organisations in relation to the domestic abuse offence, which captures patterns of coercive control, as well as physical or sexual violence and threats.  The first training statement is available at: Training Statement for the Domestic Abuse Offence | Department of Justice (justice-ni.gov.uk)I plan to publish the second training statement in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the Affordable Warmth Scheme will reopen for new approvals.
answer_text: The Affordable Warmth Scheme aims to assist low-income owner-occupiers and households in the private rented sector, with grants for energy efficiency measures.  The current Scheme is set to run until March 2026 with grants subject to the availability of funding.  As part of my initial decisions on the Departments 2024/25 budget, I have allocated £7.2m for the Affordable Warmth Scheme. This allocation includes £5.2m of commitments carried forward, leaving £2m for new approvals.My officials are now considering how this challenging position impacts the Scheme.  On 11 June 2024, I launched an Equality Impact Assessment consultation on the Department’s budget.  The consultation will end on 3 September 2024 and information from this consultation will be considered before the final budget is confirmed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what action his Department is taking to deliver the New Decade, New Approach commitment to fund three cycles of IVF treatment.
answer_text: As Minister, I want to reiterate my full support for the New Decade New Approach commitment to provide up to three funded cycles of IVF treatment for eligible women. In order to deliver on this policy funding will be essential to increase both the workforce and the physical space required to expand the service to this level, even to progress to a second cycle initially.With the budget position as it is, my Department has worked closely with colleagues in the Regional Fertility Centre to maximise the provision of fertility treatment as far as possible within existing resources. This has enabled vast improvements to waiting lists for fertility treatment as well as the recent increase in funded provision to one full IVF cycle, which will benefit approximately 150 eligible women per year, and I want to commend the Regional Fertility Centre for their work over recent years to achieve this. While this was welcome progress, I appreciate that it will not comfort those who are awaiting further treatment (after their first cycle), but unfortunately this is the maximum that can be delivered within current resources. Once funding assurances are able to be given, my Department will of course endeavour to progress delivery of this commitment as soon as feasible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on discussions between his Department and the health and social care trusts regarding settling industrial action on pay, grading and mileage allowance for domiciliary care workers.
answer_text: Under the pay settlement negotiated with Agenda for Change Trade Unions, all HSC Agenda for Change staff, including domiciliary care workers directly employed under these terms and conditions, will receive a consolidated pay uplift of 5% and a non-consolidated (pro-rata) payment of £1505.  In addition, any remaining staff in Band 1, and staff on the lower point of Band 2, will have their pay increased to match the higher pay point of Band 2.  These awards will be backdated to April 2023.  This settlement will see the restoration of pay parity with England for all health and social care staff employed under the Agenda for Change framework.As part of pay negotiations with Agenda for Change (AfC) Trade Unions, which completed on 22 February, Trade Unions, Employers and the Department have committed to work together on a number of non-pay issues, including an agreement on new AfC mileage rates.TUS have asked that the issue of mileage be dealt with under usual business at Joint Negotiation meetings to provide them the opportunity to examine any proposed options. A number of options have been presented to staff side for consideration and I am anticipating that a revised rate will be agreed within the coming weeks.Banding of all Agenda for Change roles, including those within domiciliary care, is determined by the duties and responsibilities of a particular role assessed in partnership with Trade Unions using an agreed job evaluation scheme. The banding of job roles within individual Trusts will reflect the duties and responsibilities associated with particular posts in each Trust.  Grading of domiciliary care workers’ roles has not been a feature of the discussions in regard to industrial action.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 10633/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of Rapid Response in the south Derry area of the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Rapid Response services provide urgent care to people and aim to avoid hospital admissions and enable people to live independently. The benefits of, and the need for continued development in Rapid Response services across Northern Ireland, including the south Derry area of the Northern Health and Social Care Trust, is a priority for Health and Social Care staff.There have been many developments in Rapid Response and Urgent Care services in the Northern Trust, including the recently opened new Surgical Ambulatory unit in Causeway, as well as other services, such as the Direct Assessment Unit in Antrim, the Out of Hours Marie Curie service, Phone First, to name a few.In addition, staff in the Strategic Planning and Performance Group are continuing to work with staff in NHSCT to consider the development of Acute Care at Home services for the whole of the Trust area.  However, current budgetary pressures and constraints will require any investments to be planned, within this context.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of how effectively information and best practice is shared between the five Health and Social Care Trusts in relation to complex care requirements.
answer_text: I am assured that information and best practice is effectively shared between the five Health and Social Care Trusts in relation to complex care requirements based on the systems and structures in place to support clinical decision making and maximise best patient outcomes including those living with complex care requirements. This is informed by NICE guidance which is considered best practice for Northern Ireland and other relevant standards, where appropriate.Engagement through clinical networks such as those for cancer, diabetes, stroke and cardiology supports information sharing, learning from best practice and patient lived experience to support health care professionals deliver the most appropriate care.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on Programme for Government preparations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on actions to increase provision for domiciliary care in the South Derry area of the Northern Heath and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: There have been significant efforts to increase provision of domiciliary care in both Trust Homecare and Independent Sector Providers across the Mid-Ulster area of the Northern Trust, which would include the South Derry constituency. In the Mid-Ulster area, a new Independent Sector Provider was contracted to NHSCT in October 2023 and a further new provider is in the final stages of taking up a contract. NHSCT operates an ongoing rolling recruitment programme for Trust Home Care staff, with the most recent recruitment exercise in Mid-Ulster held in March 2024. Further to this Northern Trust have completed targeted recruitment exercises in the past 12 months:In July 2023, Northern Trust completed a specific recruitment exercise with Health Sector Talent across the entire Trust Homecare service. This was successful in offering 66 successful candidates the option of contracts across Trust Homecare Service, which included posts within Mid-Ulster area. Bespoke recruitment days focused within the local communities have also been completed. The most recent recruitment day was held in Mid-Ulster locality in December 2023, where 17 successful candidates were offered the option of contracts in Trust Homecare Service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action he is taking to resolve the backlog in occupational therapy assessments for elderly patients in Mid Ulster, specifically the area covered by the Magherafelt office.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency will continue to work with SPPG and Department of Health to determine the requirement of Occupational Therapy across the region for all life stages, including elderly services.  Information specifically related to the backlog in occupational therapy assessments for elderly patients in Mid-Ulster indicates:Learning Disability Occupational TherapyLong term sickness has contributed to waiting times in this locality but it has been fully staffed since the end of January 2024.  Trust wide staffing pressures have also contributed.Community Occupational TherapyReferrals are triaged in line with regional guidance and will be seen in chronological order, with urgent referrals being prioritised. The Team is currently working to full capacity. There is no additional waiting list initiative funding which would help to bring the waiting list to within 13-week ministerial target for assessment and no investment in the service despite growing demand and complexity of work.Occupational Therapy OutpatientsThere is minimal waiting times for Recovery Service in Mid Ulster. OT Outpatients have rheumatology and neurology clinics in Magherafelt – approximately a third of those waiting in Mid Ulster at present for outpatients are older people aged  65years and older. However, staffing for these services are stretched across all areas to manage demand equitably and there has been no investment in services for many years despite growing demand.Dementia Occupational Therapy Referrals are triaged in line with access criteria. There is  a high number of priority referrals within the locality and these are usually seen within approximately 10 working days; although at times, with demands on the service, this wait can be longer. The current  waiting time is  less than 13 weeks (longest wait is 9 weeks) which is within the Ministerial target

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given that the Musgrave-based consultant who is responsible for the assessment of brain injuries is currently off on a period of indefinite leave, what actions he is taking to ensure that patients in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust can access this service.
answer_text: The NHSCT has a contract with the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust for the provision of 4 sessions per month of rehabilitation medicine to NHSCT service users who have a brain injury. This contract includes both in-reach into acute settings in Antrim and Causeway Hospitals and the review of service users in the community. Some of these service users will have been known to the Belfast Trust’s Consultant whilst they were an inpatient in the Regional Acquired Brain Injury Unit (RABIU) in Musgrave Park Hospital. Other NHSCT service users with a brain injury may have never been known to the Consultant as they did not require input from or admission to RABIU following their injury. NHSCT service users with a brain injury can also continue to receive input from NHSCT’s Community Rehabilitation Service. This service consists of various professions including Psychology, Physiotherapy, Speech and Language Therapy, Social Work, Occupational Therapy and Nursing however this service does not have a Consultant in Rehabilitation Medicine, hence the contract with BHSCT. The NHSCT does have patients who are waiting assessment for potential transfer to RABIU for an inpatient stay and remain in communication with BHSCT in regards to these patients and their plans to have them assessed.   The Team in the RABIU understand the difficulties being faced in the absence of the Consultant who would regularly see Northern Health and Social Care Trust patients.  If there is a case that the Northern Trust are concerned with, RABIU have a pathway in place where this can be escalated through the Service Management Team.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of (i) the use of reluctant discharge into contingency beds in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) whether this is an appropriate measure for the Trust to employ.
answer_text: At any one time across Northern Ireland there are a number of delayed discharges from hospital sites.  These delays affect patient flow through the hospital system and the ability of Trusts to admit sick patients into beds.  It also has a direct impact on those requiring a bed for elective surgery and negatively impacts ambulance waits at Emergency Departments as there are no beds to admit into.  Ambulances delayed at hospitals, in turn negatively impacts on the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service’s capacity to provide essential emergency care in the community.Faced with this situation all Trusts have brought into effect contingency measures, whereupon if a package of care essential to manage the person safely at home is not available, they are offered a contingency bed.    Contingency beds offer the opportunity of safe continuing care in a care home setting.  Contingency placements are provided free of charge to the service user and care home fees are waived temporarily.  The use of contingency beds is appropriate at this time and fully supported by the Department of Health.There is in Northern Ireland a regionally agreed Discharge Protocol in use in all HSC Trusts.  The Protocol considers circumstances which could cause a reluctance from the patient or their family to agree to a discharge plan.  This may be their care home of choice being unavailable or their care package being unavailable.  In cases where the package of care is unavailable there is the potential to move to a contingency bed as explained above.  In cases where the patient’s care home of choice is unavailable, patients are requested to move to another available care home bed on a temporary basis moving to their preferred choice/s when a bed is available.   This process assists patient flow through our hospital system, mitigates against delayed discharges and increases hospital capacity.  Also, to note that in recognition of the regional shortage of domiciliary care, I have recently announced a £70m support package for social care providers and hospices.  This funding will help independent sector organisations deal with rising costs from the start of the new financial year, particularly forthcoming minimum wage increases.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on plans to reintroduce the programme to Tackle Rural Poverty and Social Isolation.
answer_text: The Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation (TRPSI) programme has been ongoing since 2011. Under the current framework, which was introduced in 2016, funding of £48.4m has been provided to deliver 36 varying initiatives to address rural poverty and social isolation issues. This includes £1.57m funding in 2023/2024 with a further £0.921m, baselined in the lead delivery Department’s budgets.  I plan to consider proposals for the delivery of initiatives under the TRPSI programme for 2024/25 in conjunction with wider budget discussions to ensure funding is targeted towards key departmental priorities.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on a tourism action plan.
answer_text: Oral Question No 11 – AQO 363/22-27Tourism is truly a success story of the Good Friday Agreement. It is my intention to bring forward a Tourism Vision and Strategic Action Plan in response to my Department’s recent consultation.At last week’s Tourism NI conference, I announced that to help take this work forward I will establish a Tourism Partnership Board made up of representatives from tourism and hospitality, Executive Departments, and local government.It will help develop and oversee an Action Plan that will be co-designed and co-delivered with the industry.
