Care Opinion supports people to tell us what really matters to them, in their own words and in their own time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps he is taking to add capacity to provision of complex care in the community.
answer_text: On the 22nd March I announced an additional £70m support package for social care providers and hospices.  This funding will help independent sector organisations deal with rising costs from the start of the new financial year, particularly forthcoming minimum wage increases.  This will help stabilise the provision of key services.  To add capacity to the provision of complex care in the community will take additional funding.  As you are aware there are severe limitations on funding this year and ahead of the budget being finalised I am unable to confirm what funding has been allocated to bolster this specific area.Each of the five HSC Trusts are providing more domiciliary care than they are currently funded for and engage regularly with the independent sector to ascertain that they are maximizing the domiciliary care capacity that is in the system.  Through the Social Care Collaborative Forum there are workstreams focusing on maximizing domiciliary care and growing the domiciliary care workforce.   I recognise that the complexity of care needs in the community is growing but I am assured that the HSC Trusts are doing everything they can to ensure that an individual with complex needs is provided with the choice and support to remain living in the community if that is their choice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of complex care nursing home provision in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Northern Trust currently have 1754 people in Nursing Home Placements across all Programmes of Care. This includes residents on temporary placements and those on short respite breaks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will give direction for a regional procurement process for the provision of hairpieces, specifically in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Trusts within Northern Ireland have been provided with financial provision for the care of individuals with hair loss. This will include replacement hair services, usually wigs, for a wide range of conditions. Demand and availability of such services is determined at Trust level and arrangements are in place to offer appliances in line with locally agreed criteria. Due to challenges with the supply, quality and availability of wigs, Trusts are in the process of considering procurement options and it is planned that a regional procurement process will be taken forward by Trusts to ensure demand can be met and that wigs are of a suitable quality for patients in Northern Ireland. Once Trusts confirm the specification for wigs the tender / procurement process will be taken forward by the Business Services Organisation Procurement and Logistics Services (BSO PaLS) in conjunction with Trusts. BSO PaLS help to procure services, and develop a service contract with a provider ensuring value for money.A voucher scheme is not possible in Northern Ireland under current legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action he is taking to reinstate respite service provision for parents of adults with complex needs to the pre-pandemic level.
answer_text: DoH Adult Learning Disability officials continue to monitor respite/short break and day care provision across all Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) via monthly data returns to drive improvement. Learning Disability Services data including information regarding respite/short breaks and day care provision is analysed and published on the DOH website noted in link below: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/remobilisation-learning-disability-servicesAt 31 January 2024, using the February 2020 baseline (pre-Covid) all HSCTs are performing at over 80% in terms of the percentage of respite/short break beds available to adults with a learning disability and their parents, families/ carers.However, the demographic of service users has changed significantly and involves higher levels of complexity i.e. bariatric service users, nursing type care, increased need for ‘one to one’ supervision. This increased complexity impacts upon the number of people who can have their needs accommodated within the current service model and explains why remobilisation to 100% pre-Covid activity levels has not always been possible. To improve access to respite/short break services, support has been offered by Trusts to service users/carers in the form of Cash Grants (one off payments), cash ‘Direct payments’, Domiciliary Short Breaks and access to nursing and residential home facilities. A number of Trusts offer day support for adults with a learning disability through Shared Lives schemes. DOH officials in SPPG and Policy colleagues are working with HSCTs to ensure that the current activity is being accurately reported and monitored. DOH are reviewing the metrics and baseline used to monitor remobilisation in Learning Disability Services. This will also inform service reform as part of the Learning Disability Service Model.Families, parents/carers and service users continue to emphasise the need for a range of day opportunities, day care, respite/short break services. Trust are now delivering this via the mechanisms noted above.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to his Department's withdrawal of its Ammonia Standing Advice, (i) what engagement the Environment Agency is having with individual farmers in respect of their planning application; (ii) to detail the current interim policy on engagement with individual farmers who are impacted by this decision; and (iii) whether his Department will make the interim policy publicly available.
answer_text: (i) Since implementation of the interim case-by-case approach, NIEA has engaged with planning agents and consultants, as well as key stakeholders such as the UFU.   (ii) Queries related to individual planning applications are made via the appropriate planning authority. Due to the prioritisation of processing applications, NIEA is not in a position to deal with individual queries. iii) The interim case-by-case approach has been provided to planning consultants, planning authorities, the Planning Appeals Commission and the OEP. I hope to issue updated air quality standing advice in the near future.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many families are awaiting an Autism or ASD assessment in Mid Ulster; (ii) the average time families have spent awaiting these assessments; (iii) whether the causes of these waiting lists have been identified; and (iv) what measures his Department is putting in place to help the (a) Northern Health and Social Care Trust; and (b) the Southern Health and Social Care Trust, to reduce waiting lists for autism and ASD assessments.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many women are currently awaiting a laparoscopy as part of an endometriosis diagnosis in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust; (ii) to detail the average waiting time; and (iii) what actions are being taken to address the backlog.
answer_text: As a laparoscopy can be used for more conditions than the diagnosis of Endometriosis, it has taken some time to receive this information from Northern Trust. 110 patients are currently awaiting a laparoscopy as part of an endometriosis diagnosis and the average waiting time is 37 weeks.Northern HSC Trust have advised that in order to create additional space for Day Case surgery, bladder botox has been moved monthly from Daycase (DC)  to Outpatient with Procedure (OPP) in the Eden OPP suite. I have been advised that the Trust have plans for further daycase surgery to move to outpatient settings but this will require additional funding for resources and staffing. When the elective caesarean section list is not running an in Day Surgery, Gynaecology services request a daycase list on an acute site. Complex cases are added to the Inpatient lists and patients are considered for the independent sector if they meet the criteria and if funding is available.  Gynaecology services are planning to progress with administration and clinical validation of waiting lists, however again this is reliant on additional funding required to support administrative staff  should validation be required as a functional clinic.Covid, and general overarching pressures within the Gynaecology Service in Northern Ireland have delayed the development of Endometriosis Services. As you may be aware, my Department recently launched the Women’s Health Action Plan for Northern Ireland and we have also published the Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) Report for Gynaecology – this signals our commitment to working towards improving services for women which includes those suffering from endometriosis.It is our intention to progress the development of Endometriosis Service in the context of the GIRFT implementation plan which will be developed in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what assessment has been made on service provision for specialised endometriosis clinics.
answer_text: Covid, and general overarching pressures within the Gynaecology Service in Northern Ireland have delayed the development of Endometriosis Services. As you may be aware, my Department recently launched the Women’s Health Strategy for Northern Ireland and we have also published the Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) Report for Gynaecology – this signals our commitment to working towards improving services for women which includes those suffering from endometriosis.The GIRFT  report has identified 20 general recommendations to improve services through coordinated regional action, as well as a list of specific recommendations for each Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust to take forward. These recommendations have been accepted in principle with a view to implementing the required improvements as soon as it is feasible; however, it is acknowledged that some recommendations may require additional funding and lead-in time to deliver fully.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what statutory provisions have been made by her Department to give effect to the New Decade, New Approach commitment in relation to births, marriages and deaths to be registrable through Irish, and for wills to be validly made in Irish.
answer_text: The General Register Office currently provides the option for births, marriages and deaths to be registered with the choice of the certificate headings in English, Irish orbi-lingual English/Irish with all content in English.  This was introduced on 11 March 2022 with the implementation of the Marriage, Civil Partnership and Civil Registration (Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2022.  The Department of Finance does not hold legislative authority in relation to wills.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what steps have been taken by her Department to expand upon the provision of births, deaths and marriages being registered in Irish, and for wills to be validly made through Irish, to include (i) each being available retrospectively; and (ii) provision for forms to be made available through Irish during the registration processes.
answer_text: The General Register Office currently provides the option for births, marriages and deaths to be registered with the choice of the certificate headings in English, Irish or bi-lingual English/Irish with all content in English. This was introduced on 11 March 2022 with the implementation of the Marriage, Civil Partnership and Civil Registration (Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2022. Work towards meeting the NDNA commitment was divided into several phases, beginning with those which could be enacted most quickly. More difficult, complex and resource intensive stages would follow. Stage one of the phased approach enabled future registrations to be available in additional formats. Stage two – which would require specialist technical and legal input, resourcing and stakeholder engagement – will build on the changes introduced in Stage one by introducing registrations, certificates and forms with the headings and most of the content in English, Irish and bi-lingual English/Irish dependent on the choice of the person registering the life event. Work in relation to the commitment in the New Decade, New Approach document focussed on how future registrations might be made in Irish. Providing retrospective certificates in Irish would require careful legal and technical consideration and appropriate resources. The Department will consider how best to address these questions alongside other ambitions to progress the use of Irish in the registration services we provide. The Department of Finance does not hold legislative authority in relation to wills. I will be engaging with my Executive Colleagues to ensure this NDNA commitment is appropriately led and taken forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many people are on the social housing waiting list in Mid Ulster.
answer_text: The number of people who are on the social housing waiting list in Mid Ulster is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. You should, therefore, direct your question to the Housing Executive at its dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide the details of house building schemes for social and affordable housing in Mid Ulster, including plans for bungalow or ground floor accommodation for those with additional needs.
answer_text: As NIHE have statutory responsibility for assessing social and intermediate housing need in specific locations, including specific properties for those with additional needs, this is an operational matter for NIHE. You should, therefore, direct your question to the NIHE at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk. Local Councils will consider their own affordable housing needs for their areas, in conjunction with NIHE, through the development and delivery of Local Development Plans.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an outline of the contracts for wig provision in each of Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Trusts within Northern Ireland have been provided with financial provision for the care of individuals with hair loss.  This will include replacement hair services, usually wigs, for a wide range of conditions.  Availability of such services is determined at Trust level and arrangements are in place to offer appliances in line with locally agreed criteria.However as there have been ongoing issues with the quality and availability of wigs, Trusts are in the process of considering procurement options around the supply, quality and availability of wigs. It is planned that this will be taken forward as a regional procurement process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his plans for social and affordable housing in Mid Ulster.
answer_text: As NIHE have statutory responsibility for assessing social and intermediate housing need in specific locations this is an operational matter for NIHE. You should, therefore, direct your question to the NIHE at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk. Local Councils will consider their own affordable housing needs for their areas, in conjunction with NIHE, through the development and delivery of Local Development Plans.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the accessibility of public transport.
To ask the Minister for Communities what provision there is for emergency housing accommodation in Mid Ulster, including in rural areas.
answer_text: Provision of emergency housing accommodation is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  You should therefore direct your question to the Housing Executive’s dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has had any correspondence with the Public Health Agency regarding the funding for Ageing Well services.
answer_text: I have had no recent correspondence with the Public Health Agency (PHA) specifically regarding funding for the Ageing Well services.However, I can confirm that there are a number of schemes in operation to support Ageing Well services, which are funded by the PHA. The PHA has adopted a life course approach with specific investment targeted to support the health and wellbeing of older adults, to improve health outcomes and narrow the gaps in life expectancy and healthy years. Programmes in place to support Ageing Well include:Home Accident Prevention (£560k, regional programme)Intergenerational Practice (£33k regional programme)Arts in Health (£176k, Regional Programme)In addition, there have also been a number of Age Friendly investments specific to the Mid Ulster Council Area including: World Health Organisation Age Friendly Communities Programme (£50k per annum)Community Planning Age Well Partnership (£45k per annum) Addressing the needs of older people (£77k per annum)Make a Change Programme / Joint working Engagement Project, (£43k per annum)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the School Enhancements Projects works in Mid Ulster which had been agreed during the last mandate but have been subject to pause since.
answer_text: There are currently six School Enhancement Programme (SEP) projects in the Mid Ulster constituency.  Despite the extremely challenging capital budget position, budget was made available to progress all SEP projects through design and planning approval to pre-tender stage.  Once a project reaches pre-tender stage, a decision to release to tender is taken on a case-by-case basis, dependent on the budget available at that time.  Due to the constrained budget position, no construction contracts were awarded for SEP projects in the current financial year. St John's Primary School, Coalisland, was announced in May 2018. The proposed project involves a new build extension to provide classrooms and associated ancillary spaces and a refurbishment of part of the existing school building. The construction tender has been assessed and the contract will be awarded when budget availability is confirmed. St Joseph's Grammar School, Donaghmore, was announced in May 2018. The proposed project includes a new build block to provide new School Meals Accommodation, a library and teaching accommodation and a new 3G pitch. The project is currently at RIBA Stage 2 (Concept Design).Dungannon Primary School was announced in January 2019. The proposed project includes a new build extension and reconfiguration of some existing areas of the school. The business case for this project is currently being developed. Kilronan School, Magherafelt, was announced in January 2019. The proposed project aims to deliver a single storey extension to provide Post Primary and Post 16 Accommodation, internal reconfiguration works and removal of double mobile and single modular units. RIBA Stage 4 (Technical design) has commenced and will take this project up to pre-tender stage. St Brigid’s Primary School Mayogall, Magherafelt was announced in May 2020. The proposed project aims to deliver an extension, alterations, and refurbishment to existing single storey school. The scoping of this project is currently being reviewed following comments received from the school.St Peter’s Primary School, Moortown, was announced in May 2020.  The school’s enrolment number is currently being reviewed by the Council for Catholic Maintained Schools as the school’s actual enrolment is less than 85% of the approved enrolment and therefore fails one of the Gateway checks in the SEP protocol.Due to the current constraints on the capital budget, SEP projects will be progressed to pre-tender stage and a decision to release to tender will be taken based on the budget available at that time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of staffing levels within treatment rooms across the Northern Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) whether he plans to increase the budget to allow for recruitment in this area.
answer_text: There are 46 Treatment Rooms within the NHSCT with allocated funding for 97.59wte staff.  At 13 February 24, there were 87.6wte treatment room staff in post which would indicate a vacancy level of 9.99wte.  Treatment Room Band 5 and Band 3 posts are recruited on rolling recruitment programmes with adverts ‘live’ all the time and interviews scheduled every 8 weeks for Band 5 applicants and 12 weeks for Band 3 applicants. The next Band 5 interviews are scheduled for late February 24 the next Band 3 interviews are scheduled for March 24.Budgets for Northern Health and Social Trust (NHSCT) treatment room services are held by the NHSCT.  Whilst some GP practices have taken treatment room staff employed by the NHSCT under direct employment, the bulk of the budget remains with the NHSCT.  While the Department will always carefully consider requests for additional funding across health and social care, a budget for 2024/25 has not yet been set it is expected that the overall financial position for all NI departments will remain very challenging and therefore no commitments can be made at this stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) what correspondence he has had with the Irish government regarding the Shared Island Fund; and (ii) what plans he has for collaboration to deliver funding to local community groups and sporting clubs.
answer_text: I have not corresponded with the Irish government in relation to the Shared Island Fund. I look forward to working with the widest range of funding partners where there is an opportunity to improve investment in communities in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the travel and mileage rates for domiciliary care workers in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust in (a) statutory services; and (b) the independent sector allowance; and (ii) whether he plans to give direction for increases to both.
answer_text: The current travel and mileage rates for domiciliary care workers employed directly by the Northern Health and Social Care Trust and all other HSC trusts are outlined below;56 pence per mile for the first 3,500 miles;30 pence per mile for each mile thereafter.Trade Union representatives have requested a discussion on appropriate mileage rates as part of the wider discussions to settle industrial action. Officials from my Department have commenced this engagement which will inform any final decision on mileage rates.Unlike the statutory sector, there is no regional rate for mileage in regard to independent sector providers, which is part of the terms and conditions offered by individual employers. The Department cannot mandate what levels of pay, or terms and conditions private employers should offer, including in relation to mileage or travel costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many patients are on a waiting list for a wig as a result of hair loss, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Information on patients who are on a waiting list for a wig as a result of hair loss is not available centrally in the Department.  This information has been sourced from the Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts.The Belfast HSC Trust indicated that patients referred are given an appointment and that the majority get their wig on the day. There are <5 patients waiting for their wig after their appointment.The Southern HSC Trust reported that there are 38 patients on a waiting list for a wig.The Northern, South Eastern and Western HSC Trusts do not record waiting lists for a wig, as patients are given a wig at an appointment during the referral stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 16629/17-22, whether he plans to introduce the new colon capsule endoscopy, as trialled in England.
answer_text: The HSC continues to explore advances in technology and innovative ways to meet the healthcare needs of the population. Colon capsule endoscopy is a promising new technology as a gastrointestinal diagnosis tool. A number of Trusts in Northern Ireland deliver colon capsule endoscopy on a small scale for specific patient types. The potential to expand this capacity to all Trusts was last discussed at the Regional Endoscopy Reform and Modernisation Group (RERMG) in May 2022. It was subsequently felt that expansion of this at scale would be prohibitive from a cost perspective when compared to standard endoscopy.  There have been CCE pilots in health services across the UK, but evidence is still emerging to inform future practice. It is important to note that colonoscopy is still the gold standard for colorectal cancer diagnosis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the current provision of respite services for adults with additional complex needs in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) how his Department plans to increase provision for families who have seen their hours reduce in recent years.
answer_text: (i) The provision of high quality, regular respite/short breaks provision remains the most frequent request from carers and a top priority for the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.Currently the Northern Trust Learning Disability Services have commissioned and offer 12 residential and 2 nursing respite/short break beds, based on 24-hour, 7-day service delivery. The Trust currently deliver 12 out of the 14 respite/short break beds via Trust led services. None of these beds are currently blocked, facilitating access for service users in line with their assessed needs to provide support to carers and families.The Trust currently offers on average 16 nights per year to families in these respite/short break services.The Northern Trust assessment of need indicates that this delivery falls short of the current demand for respite/short breaks. The Trust have an increasing and pressing need for respite/short break facilities for individuals with complex needs (1:1 / 2:1). These facilities require more room space, quieter environments and staff trained in intervention techniques. In addition, the Trust have increasing levels of demand for standard residential respite/short breaks and have calculated that in order to meet demand arising via children’s transitions and existing carer needs, it requires an additional two complex needs respite/short break beds and six residential respite/short break beds.This shortfall has led to the Trust having to source ad hoc respite/short break care to meet critical needs, at financial risk and beyond available funding. The Trust are open to delivery of respite/short break services through a mixed model of delivery (Independent Sector / Trust led) based on the quality, location and value for money provided.(ii) A Task & Finish Group has been established to finalise the Adult Learning Disability Service Model (LDSM), finalise the Children with Disabilities (CwD) Framework, and to develop costed implementation plans.A review of HSC Services was undertaken by SPPG in September 2023, which found variation in the type and level of services available for people with Learning Disabilities, carers and families.Subsequently, a review of evidence and research was conducted to identify best practice, which included services to meet the needs of carers and families.This evidence base was used to develop a revised draft of the Learning Disability Service Model in December 2023, which has been shared with Trusts, the independent sector and a sample of family groups for feedback to inform future service provision. Further revisions are being made with a view to wider engagement in the coming months. The Learning Disability Service Model is the regional road map for the future of Learning Disability Services.SPPG continue to obtain data from Health and Social Care Trusts regarding respite/short break and day care provision to drive forward a return to pre-Covid service levels in all Trusts and to ensure alternative service provisi

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the current provision of respite services for children with additional complex needs in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) how his Department plans to increase provision for families who have seen their hours reduce in recent years.
answer_text: (i)  The Department would acknowledge that there are current significant regional pressures regarding the short break availability provision for families who have children with additional complex needs. Access to Short Break and Respite Services is assessed by each HSC Trust, and is determined by individually assessed needFor those children with a disability, Short Break provision is delivered under a Self-Directed Support Framework. This comprises of a range of Direct Services provided by the Trust (Short Break Residential Units & Sharing the Care Short Breaks Service), Managed Budgets, Direct Payments and mixed packages comprising two or more of these - depending on parental/carer choice in consideration of the assessed needs of the child. Direct Services may be delivered by the Trust or via an organisation contracted by the Trust. Currently, there are two residential Short Break Residential Units for children with disabilities in the Northern Trust (Whitehaven Residential Short Break Unit and Rainbow Lodge Short Break Unit). Whitehaven Residential Short Break Unit has a current maximum capacity of between 3 and 5 children per night.  However, the capacity of the unit may on occasions be reduced, dependant on the complexity of the needs of the group of children attending, the staffing ratio required and the training of the staff available to meet the children’s complex health needs, and compatibility of the group of children. Rainbow Lodge Short Break Unit (Praxis run facility for NHSCT) has a capacity of between 2 and 3 children per night for short break respite care. However, between October 2021 & February 2023 all Short Break provision at Rainbow Lodge Short Break Unit was closed by RQIA.  The unit re-opened in February 2023 but since this time has operated at a 50% reduced capacity. Rainbow Lodge Short Break Unit closed again in May 2023 due to staffing issues, and this Short Break Unit currently remains closed. The Trust intends to take over the running of Rainbow Lodge directly to improve service provision.The Northern Trust continue to develop their ‘Sharing The Care Short Break Service’ to meet changing need and ensure a higher short break provision across the Trust. The Northern Trust’s ‘Sharing The Care Short Break Service’ currently provides befriending service, summer activity groups and specialist short break fostering placements for disabled children. In addition, the Northern Trust utilise approximately 10 placements for children within Sense Nursery via a contract, which provides specialist brief short break input for pre-school children who meet the criteria. The Northern Trust also access group-based activity support for children via the Cedar Foundation in a 12-week rolling programme(ii) In terms of improving service provision, the Department of Health has a system to measure and support the improvement of performance of each HSC Trust throughout the year.  To assist with improving the quality of respite service prov

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for his assessment of the current provision of special educational needs placements within Mid Ulster; and (ii) whether he plans to increase this provision.
answer_text: There are significant pressures in the Mid Ulster area in relation to specialist education provision currently provided in Kilronan Special School (SS) and through specialist provision in mainstream schools (SPiMS) classes in 12 primary and seven post-primary schools.The Education Authority (EA) is working at pace to confirm demand for specialist education provision, to establish more places in special schools and to create more SPiMS for the 2024/25 academic year.  Plans are in place to provide further specialist education provision at Magherafelt Primary School (PS) for September 2024.  Schools in the Mid Ulster constituency will be contacted over the next few weeks to discuss the possibility of establishing additional SPiMS classes for September 2024.   The EA is in the process of acquiring additional land adjacent to Kilronan SS, which will enable expansion of the school in the medium to long-term to meet the increased demand for places.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on when capital funding opportunities can be expected from Sport NI.
answer_text: Sport NI currently has no open capital funding opportunities; however they are currently administering four capital investment programmes where funding was secured during 2023/24.   Sport NI has bid for funding to deliver further capital investment in 2024/25 through the Renewable Energy Fund; Your School, Your Club; and Safety at Sports Grounds.  These investment programmes are subject to budget availability.  In addition, Sport NI are currently exploring options for a new Lottery Investment programme, aimed at developing infrastructure which increases participation in sport and physical activity.  Sport NI publish all new funding opportunities via their website and social media channels; I would recommend that sporting clubs and organisations sign up to these platforms to ensure they are made aware of any new opportunities as they arise.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has corresponded with CCEA regarding amendments to grading criteria for this year's cohort of GCSE & A-level students, in consideration of lost teaching hours relating to strike action.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland, as throughout the UK, external examination provision is overseen by the Joint Council for Qualifications (JCQ), of which CCEA is a constituent member. JCQ provides guidance to examination centres (schools and FE settings) stipulating requirements in relation to matters pertaining to examinations such as GCSEs, AS level and A levels. JCQ guidance covers such issues as special consideration when performance in an exam has been impacted by illness or other exceptional reasons.In accordance with the JCQ requirements for examination centres, schools and colleges across the UK are required to have a contingency plan which addresses circumstances such as lost teaching and learning. The contingency plan outlines the centre’s responsibility and where there is disruption to teaching it remains the responsibility of schools to put in place alternative arrangements to ensure learners are prepared for examinations.It is not appropriate for the Department to intervene in these arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on any recruitment drives for domiciliary care workers in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The NHSCT continues to operate an ongoing rolling recruitment programme in their Homecare Service. Further to this the Trust have engaged in a number of targeted recruitment exercises.In July 2023, the NHSCT completed a specific recruitment exercise with Health Sector Talent which was successful in offering 66 successful candidates the option of contracts in the Trust’s Homecare Service. The Trust advises that significant recruitment challenges have continued to persist within some localities in the Northern Trust area. In these areas, the plan is that bespoke recruitment days focused in the local community will be completed. This was most recently held on 2 December 2023 in the Mid-Ulster area. While recruitment continues to be very challenging area, it remains an area of focus for the Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many people in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust are awaiting a care package that has been assessed as necessary but is still outstanding.
answer_text: As at the 31st January 2024 in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust there were:1,157 Clients Assessed and waiting for a FULL PACKAGE of Domiciliary Care553 Clients Assessed and waiting for a PARTIAL PACKAGE of Domiciliary CareDomiciliary care services continue to experience significant pressures across the region, and there are a range of factors currently impacting upon delivery of this service; this includes significant retention issues in regard to domiciliary care staff.Where delivery of care packages has been negatively impacted due to challenges regarding capacity, Trusts have in place mitigation measures to try to ensure services continue to be provided.My Department remains committed to the provision of a high quality domiciliary care service, and will continue to work alongside Trusts and independent sector providers to maintain and support service provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many people, in each of the last five years, in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust have passed away before the package of care they required was delivered.
answer_text: Information on domiciliary care packages which are requested but not put in place due to the death of the service user, is not collected by the Department or by HSC Trusts. The exact information might only be obtained through a manual trawl requiring extensive and disproportionate labour at significant cost.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the criteria that must be met in order for a person to qualify for hours of sits in their home.
answer_text: The allocation of respite sits in an individual’s own home is determined by a holistic based approach to the individual’s assessment of need and risk. The Trusts are guided by the following regional Circulars:Circular HSC (ECCU) 2010, DOHSSPS, Care Management, Provision of Services and Charging Guidance; and Circular HSS (ECCU) 2008: Regional Access Criteria for Domiciliary Care .An individual assessment of need determines the need for services. Trusts will explore suitable services following the key elements of community care provision and delivery, one element being Respite Care. Under Respite Care, a domiciliary care service or sitting service at home can be arranged but no specific criteria is applied and is informed by an assessment of need/risk assessment. Circular HSS (ECCU) 2008: Regional Access Criteria for Domiciliary Care  provides a framework for a consistent approach to eligibility and fair access to domiciliary care. The criteria for a domiciliary care service; sitting service falls under non-residential respite care. Trusts will determine which services can be provided to individuals, following a risk assessment. This is applied for all domiciliary care service provision, which includes personal care, practical care, respite care, day care and will depend on the resources available.If the individual risk assessment identifies a critical or substantial risk to independence and help cannot be sourced from elsewhere, individuals will be assessed as eligible for a domiciliary care service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many accessible rooms in total are available within domestic violence refuges.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised that it does not hold information on how many accessible rooms are available within domestic violence refuges. The relevant information would be held by each Scheme provider.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what measures are in place to ensure that maternity services are accessible for deaf and disabled women.
answer_text: Maternity Services currently take reasonable and necessary steps to ensure that appropriate care and information is provided to deaf and disabled pregnant women. This includes women with a learning disability, those with a sensory disability and women with a physical disability.All appointments for maternity are in Health and Social Care (HSC) premises which are accessible for women with physical disabilities.All information shared with women will be discussed at antenatal care visits. Key health messages are designed with low literacy in mind. Further, it is recognised that women with a learning disability may be unable to read and/or understand all the information contained in health promotion literature. In these cases midwives/health professionals will discuss the literature with women as it is given to them to ensure it is understood. Women who are deaf have appointments arranged with an interpreter present.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what measures are in place to ensure that domestic violence helplines are accessible to women who are (i) deaf; (ii) blind or have visual impairments; and (iii) non-verbal or who experience communication challenges.
answer_text: The 24hr Domestic and Sexual Abuse Helpline provides information, advice and support to all those affected by domestic and sexual abuse across Northern Ireland.  This is inclusive of those who are deaf, blind or have communication challenges. For those who are deaf, or experience communication challenges, there is a web chat facility available with email functionality.  We have seen a significant increase is the use of this over recent years.  The helpline also regularly uses a translator service.  When required they link in with other services to assist in making referrals with the caller’s permission.   A person could also call the Helpline on someone else’s behalf, in order to obtain advice and support where there are accessibility difficulties Dependant on the nature of the caller’s enquiry, callers are regularly signposted to relevant agencies.  More generally, the Helpline operator will be engaging with stakeholders about how they can improve accessibility of the Helpline and are open to suggestions in this regard.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many staff are employed at Brackenburn Clinic; and (ii) for his assessment of whether this meets the level of need.
answer_text: The current staffing provision in the Brackenburn Clinic is 1 part-time Locum Consultant Psychiatrist, 1.4 (WTE) Nurse Therapist and 1 (WTE) Administrative Support.  This is not an adequate staffing resource to meet the level of demand on Brackenburn’s services. In addition, key staff have left the service due to retirement or career progression and repeated attempts by the Belfast HSC Trust to recruit to the service have been unsuccessful. It is for these reasons that in April 2019 my Department asked the then Health and Social Care Board to conduct a review of Gender Identity Services. The report and implementation plan have recently been submitted to my Department for consideration. My final decision on a future model for the service will be informed by a full public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether data collected by the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency to inform policy development within his Department is disaggregated by Section 75 characteristics.
answer_text: My Department routinely uses disaggregated Section 75 data collected by the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA) to inform policy development. My Department commissions NISRA to carry out the Travel Survey for Northern Ireland and also commissions modules on walking, cycling, public transport, road safety and e-cars in NISRA’s Continuous Household Survey. Within these surveys, data is collected on age, sex, marital status, ethnicity, religion, dependents, limiting long-term illness and sexual identity. My Department uses these data, as well as other Section 75 data available from NISRA, i.e. Census data, to inform policy development and for equality screening and monitoring purposes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether data collected by the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency to inform policy development within her Department is disaggregated by Section 75 characteristics.
answer_text: The Department of Education uses a variety of data collected by NISRA (Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency) for policy development in addition to other non NISRA data sources. The collection of equality data and it's disaggregation and publication by NISRA is a policy matter for the Department of Finance.NISRA statisticians, within the Department of Education, collect and collate information for the Department from a range of sources including surveys and administrative databases.  The range of Section 75 information that can be made available for policy development may differ in each source depending on the nature of the data collection itself.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether data collected by the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency to inform policy development within his Department is disaggregated by Section 75 characteristics.
answer_text: My Department uses a variety of data collected by the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA), including data that are disaggregated by Section 75 characteristics, for policy development. The collection of equality data by NISRA and their disaggregation and publication is a policy matter for the Department of Finance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether data collected by the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency to inform policy development within his Department is disaggregated by Section 75 characteristics.
answer_text: The Department uses a variety of data collected by the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA) for policy development. The collection of equality data and its disaggregation and publication by NISRA is a policy matter for the Department of Finance. NISRA statisticians, within the Department of Health, collect and collate information from a range of sources including primary surveys and administrative databases.  The range of Section 75 information that can be made available for policy development may differ in each source depending on the nature of the data collection itself.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether data collected by the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency to inform policy development within her Department is disaggregated by Section 75 characteristics.
answer_text: The collection of equality data and it's disaggregation and publication by NISRA is a policy matter for the Department of Finance. Data from surveys collected by NISRA and released by my Department as official statistics are disaggregated by equality categories where sample sizes are large enough to provide reliable estimates.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what mechanisms are in place for transgender individuals to avail of fertility preservation before transitioning.
answer_text: The Regional Fertility Centre (RFC) in Belfast Health and Social Care Trust is able to assist individuals who wish to avail of fertility preservation before transitioning. Adults who are being treated by the Gender Identity Service at Brackenburn Clinic for gender dysphoria or young people who are being treated by the Knowing Our Identity Service can be referred to the RFC for fertility preservation before commencing hormone therapy or preparing for gender reassignment surgery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the Childcare Strategy.
answer_text: I am committed to progressing the Executive Childcare Strategy as quickly as possible.  My Department has launched a tender exercise to commission an independent research report on a Review of Childcare Services in NI.  It is envisaged that a contract to take forward the review will be in place by end of June 2022 and will run to December 2022.	Work is also ongoing to establish a childcare stakeholder engagement forum.  My officials intend to contact sectoral representatives over the summer months, with a view to convening the first meeting of the group shortly thereafter.		Ultimately, the timeline for publication and implementation of the Executive Childcare Strategy requires Executive agreement and approval on significant policy decisions, including agreement on the overall budget required for childcare.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether LGBTQI+ specific care is included in training for GPs.
answer_text: The three-year General Practice training programme is administered on my Department’s behalf by the Northern Ireland Medical and Dental Training Agency.The core course curriculum includes six Professional Topic Guides, one of which is equality, diversity and inclusion, encompassing LGBTQI+ issues and the GP’s role in reducing discrimination and enhancing inclusion.The curriculum also covers a wide range of clinical topics which address LGBTQI+ issues such as reproductive medicine and sexual health.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to expand access to IVF treatment for lesbian and bisexual couples, who cannot access treatment on the same basis as heterosexual couples.
answer_text: The access criteria for IVF in Northern Ireland were already expanded in June 2019.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many people are waiting to be seen by Brackenburn Clinic.
answer_text: Brackenburn Clinic in Belfast HSC Trust is a location that provides psychosexual services regionally for Northern Ireland, within the psychological therapies service. The total number of people waiting for psychosexual services as at 31 April 2022 was 566.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the change to the Bereavement Support Payment criteria in respect of cohabiting couples with children will come into effect.
answer_text: In order to allow payment of a Bereavement Support Payment to cohabiting couples with children, a Remedial Order is being brought forward. I anticipate that it will come into effect later this year. My officials are working closely with DWP colleagues to ensure that people here will benefit from this change in policy at the earliest opportunity.
